text: Very beautiful museum with cat art. However, it is quite small and lot of the art is just posters etc so I think it is not worth the money. With a cheaper ticket price I would highly recommend.
So enjoyed our visit. The house is gorgeous as are the real and not real cats! Don't forget to look up at the ceilings.

Personally, I really enjoyed the place. It is entirely dedicated to cats, through artworks and artistic objects, and you also have the opportunity to visit a beautiful 17th-century house overlooking the canals of Amsterdam.Unfortunately, however, this was not enough to make up for the cold and distant attitude of the two young women at the ticket desk. Kindness is always desirable in public places such as museums, but in a place devoted to the love of other living beings, a warm and friendly welcome should almost be a given. Sadly, this was not the case—neither upon arrival nor when paying for the book and posters we purchased in the bookshop.I don’t think Mr. Meijer would be pleased, but I do believe the place is still worth visiting for lovers of these wonderful felines and for art enthusiasts.

Really expensive as you can spend little more than 20 minutes here.   The lady on entrance was not overly friendly or welcoming.  We saw one real cat and there seemed to be lots that you couldn’t get into.  So t waste your money give it to a cat rescue instead!
Son was looking forward to visiting but left really disappointed. 3 small rooms, small garden. Took 15 mins to walk round. 25 euros for 2 people.
Nice little museum focussing on cats. Outside space was a bonus and the exhibits are nicely displayed.Building it is housed in is beautiful.

Dog-lovers will probably not get much out of the Katten Kabinet.Cat-lovers, by contrast, should arrange to be met as they leave by a sensible friend who will stop them from heading for the nearest Cat Sanctuary with adoption in mind.The house itself is a treat, and the several rooms open to visitors celebrate the Cat in its many wonderful guises.There is fine art: cats sneaking into a sketch by Rembrandt, a poster by Toulouse-Lautrec, a Picasso, the magnificent Barbershop by Abraham Teniers and a tribute to a TS Eliot poem. And there is low culture: cats in the context of panto, pinball, advertising and satire.You will find ceramic, mummified, glass, hieratic and many other types of cat in many different postures. On a seriously clawed sofa you may even encounter a real cat, licking itself in that irresistible leg-of-mutton way they have.The Katten Kabinet is outstanding. No-one with any feeling for cats should miss it. And the ticket price is only half what the Rijksmuseum (almost completely bereft of cats) charges.

it's a small museum with displays of cat related pieces, there are also 3 cats in the museum. didn't spend much time there since it's a small place, and also the ticket is actually pretty expensive even with the discount from the "i amsterdam card".

Really interesting cat themed art, including Rembrandt and Picasso pieces (everyone loves cats!) Take heed of the sign at the reception clearly stating you should not touch the black cat, and do so at your own risk, I took great pleasure in sitting next to them and watching them scratch guests who hadn’t seen the sign. Very grand building, lovely art work, not too expensive in the gift shop, but importantly, two very beautiful resident felines.

This is a small site so easily viewed. Most cat paintings, posters, and sculptures that I have seen in one location. There are carpeted stairs to the viewings and a lovely garden with posters out back. If you turn left when exiting the house and take the 1st left at the intersection, you can walk to the flower market. Walk across the bridge by the flower market, and you can visit the mouse house which is at a safe distance from the Kat Kabinet.

My cat loving teens enjoyed this visit but I found it very expensive for such a small display. We spent no more than 20 minutes here and walked around very slowly. It is a corridor and two rooms with picture/ornaments of cats. What is there is unique and quirky but I do not think the price is appropriate. It cost our family over 50 euros - it was more than the Van Gogh museum!

Cats cats cats, one person's obsession turned into an eclectic mix of artifacts of all types, its small but packed little musems, in a beautiful house opposite a canal, even if you are not a cat person this quirky little charm is well worth supporting, you may meet a resident cat too.

We who thought we would spend an hour seeing + in the museum, that it was not our disappointment to discover the three small rooms accessible, we spent maximum 15 minutes and still sat in the garden... besides do not expect to see cats!! Only one was present and again he tried to scratch after 2 minutes!! Speaking of which! Only a mini poster warns you not to pet it, so if you do not go to the checkout, or you do not pay attention you will get scratched because no one warns!!! Visible litter gives off a smell + strong cat smell... in short pass your way especially for this price

Visited the cat museum today and really enjoyed it! Some of the bad reviews seem to come from people who struggle with their reading comprehension skills - this is a museum, not a petting zoo or cat café. The museum is within a stunning apartment by a canal with beautiful architecture and details and full of interesting historical paintings and various cat memoriabilia. There are even a few cats walking around or snoozing inside. There is even a peaceful and pretty garden in the back. It's fairly small and provides maybe 30-60 minutes of entertainment but I really enjoyed it and very happy that I visited it. I don't understand the people complaining, as said this place promises a collection of cat related art and that's exactly what you get. I also loved the opportunity to even just visit the inside of one of theses beautiful houses in the city centre.

If you really like cats, this is the place for you.  All sorts of art pieces dedicated to cats.  Very interesting.  We have had many cats and this place does the species justice.  There's even a real cat or two roaming around the premises.  Outdoor garden as well.

Truly a disappointment, 2 rooms with paintings depicting cats and photographs of characters with their cats, 2/3 cats spinningPrice 12.50 € per person, at least part of the cost went to feline colonies, cat associations...Money thrown away

TOURIST TRAP - Extremely disappointing; mainly if you are going with your children.  Only one cat around (let’s be honest, we heard some hens clucking in the yard!).  They charge €12.5 for a few decrepit premises with very old cat-themed prints and paintings hung on the wall and non-sense memorabilia. Truly, our worst experience in town.

Nice museum. Beautiful house, typical Dutch architecture, full of sculptures, drawings, paintings, posters and photos of cats. It also has a mummy covering several centuries of history. It's worth checking out.

My husband and I are are crazy cat people, so when in Amsterdam, of course we had to visit Kattenkabinet (The Cat Cabinet), a museum all about CATS!  Kattenkabinet is a quirky museum with an eclectic collection of cat related objects such as paintings, figurines, and even a mummified cat from 200 BC.  And of course there are actual cats living in the museum.If you like cats, Kattenkabinet is a must see!

The Cat Cabin Museum in Amsterdam is a cute and cozy spot, with charming decor and an impressive collection of cat-themed pictures and artwork. However, it’s a bit disappointing for cat lovers, as there was only one cat present during my visit. While the ambiance is delightful, more feline companions would make the experience much better.

Good fun I’d you’ve done the Rijksmuseum, the Van Gogh or MOCO but there’s not much in there to keep you entertained for more than 20 minutes or so, I saw an actual cat in there for all of about ten seconds before they ran off but if you’re worried that it might be too much cat capers for the human mind to tolerate, there are also monkeys and dogs making cameo appearances (along with Lenin, Hemingway and Dali)

I love cats, but this is just a waste of money. A  few cat pictures and two live cats. I had to walk around it twice to make sure I didn't miss something, but no, that is it. A few cat pictures (all found on the internet anyway) and two sleeping cats. That was it. Massively disappointing. A pure tourist trap and one of the most expensive 20 minutes of my life.
A lot Picture of cats- that’s all 🧐 the ticket was expensive enough - no real cats - and no good deals about the pictures or interesting stories

This was an absolutely fantastic experience in such a beautiful building with a lovely quirky collection, we even saw a tuxedo cat and a calico cat! The art was amazing and the story behind this museum is lovely

I have a love for cats so knew I wanted to try this place out. Entry is a fair price and there is basically a house and garden to explore and see lots of different cat artworks, status and pictures. This was all very interesting and I especially enjoyed seeing the mummified cat which is something I’ve never seen before. However, based on reviews and the many litter trays dotted about the house, there were no cats. We went about an hour before closing time and it seemed the cats had had their fill for the day and had gone to bed. Not the worst thing in the world, but it would’ve been nice to see them. Overall, I think if you’re a cat lover, you’ll enjoy this. Otherwise, I think it’ll be a bit underwhelming.

KattenKabinet is a cat lover’s paradise, consisting of several rooms of eclectic cat art, including a shrine where you can leave a message to your own cat. When we visited, there were also a few actual cats roaming around! This is definitely a quick visit, but a fun stop in. Bonus - you get a little peek into a beautiful canal house! I think some people might think this is overpriced, but I really enjoyed my brief stop here.

If you’re going primarily to see the actual cats you will be disappointed. The cat art and history  is great, but it is clearly advertised as being like “full of cats”. There was 1 cat and he immediately tried to scratch and bite me ☹️ 100% not worth the money.
Amazing place. Has loads of cat themed artworks and decor. Overall we really enjoyed the chill vibe as you explore. Also there is real cats that you get to say hi too! :)
Lovely fun little museum, lots to read about and see, from paintings, scultures and advertising, a must for cat lovers visting Amsterdam

Very nice museum with a lovely array of cat things to look at, there are live cats in there although we didn’t see them :( there is also a garden but that was closed during our visit (April) it opens during nicer weather. It is quite small so you can get around it fast but we paid €5 and felt it was certainly well worth the priceThey don’t allow bags to be taken round but they let you leave it behind reception and give you a little cat card with a number on so you don’t take another persons bag.Loved it in here, the building was also very lovely, will have to visit again in the warmer months for the garden, and hopefully see the resident cats :)
A great hidden spot along Amsterdam's canals - a must see if you are a cat lover like myself. More of a cat-shrine than museum but still a great visit and worth the trip!

A special museum. Not only because you enter such a stately canal house, but also the reason for this museum, the noble hangover JP Morgan. The museum is small, i.e. about three ‘rooms’ so to speak large rooms. Those rooms are already beautiful to see because of the grandeur of such an old building. People who claim and report to have been outside within 15 minutes have sold themselves short. Everything you see has to do with cats. From advertising posters, paintings to drawings and photos of acquaintances from home and abroad with their beloved four-legged friend. You can indeed run through it but then you miss the sweet notes from the owners to their sweet cat, the meal of Choupette by Karl Lagerfeld or the poems of Wolkers, tableware, and much more beautiful. If you expect a cat café then yes, but it is also a museum... Last week we were allowed to pet one of the present gentlemen of the house who was flattered on a couch, and he purred that it was good... Price is on the Amsterdam side and a bit high euro 12,50 but och for a cup of coffee you pay nowadays.... and you enjoy that less long and after.

You can enjoy antiques and posters related to cats. While the exhibition isn't very large, it's understandable given the small rooms. There are also posters in the backyard, where you can take a refreshing break while viewing them. Some reviews mention 'not seeing any cats,' but it's important to remember that the cats aren't employees. Those who are observant might spot a cat hiding in the backyard. If you're lucky, you may even get to pet one dozing peacefully on the floor.

I was disappointed, it was quite expensive for what it was…. Only took 20 mins to go through…but as we only went to kill some time it served a purpose. There isn’t much to read to tell you about the art and the artist. It is also very small! I didn’t even see any of the real cats people mentioned! I love cats but this was a bit boring! I wanted to learn something! Some cool cat pieces though!
It was very nice and I liked that they had 5 cats they were friendlies but the only problem was that it was very tiny but it's still a little cool experience.
it’s really nice but it’s a bit too expensive :/we loved the artwork but it’s so hot in there and not that many rooms, the real cats are the highlight

It was a fun experience and for a £5 student ticket not too bad however there were only 2 cats and it took us a total 30 minutes to finish the entire thing. Can’t say I would do it again but it was quite fun.

I was so excited when havign found this museum and honestly veyr excited to meet the cats especially. The art is pretty no lie but ALL the cats except for one was there and honestly this is very disappointing as the msueum is shocasing the cats wandering around the rooms (even if they have a character and do not allow to be petted) but they're still there. For that reason I do not recommend it and would not go back, and I feel it was way too overpriced for what it offers in reality.

I visited on a Thursday morning. It started off quiet but the traffic picked up. There were two cats in residence that were delightful! On view for visitors included artwork, sculptures, and all sorts of knick knacks. There was a garden but it was closed off due to renovations.

Interesting museum and a must see for any cat lovers. However there were only a couple rooms open so there wasn’t much to see and we unfortunately didn’t see the cats :( Other than that we had a lovely visit. Staff were very friendly and helpful

A small museum dedicated to cats, in the form of posters, advertisements, paintings, statues...It is done fairly quickly, in about 30 minutes, including a short tour through the garden. Perfect for cat lovers, admission is 10€. We were able to cross the path of the matous who inhabit the premises. Small friendly activity to do if you have some free time.

This was a fun and cozy museum. Lots of art about cats and with cats in. We also saw a cat, but it was so popular that we were not greeted. Very cool that they have kept the room and interior a little like it was original, so you get to see a little how these old houses look inside too. Recommend a visit.

This “cabinet” or museum is recommended only when you really do not have access to the Internet where you can google some cat portraits of (famous) people with (their) cats… that was the main room of this “cabinet”. There are no tickets when you pay the fee 🤔 Would not come again but you can give a try to it 😉

The beautiful old building houses a fun and different exhibition of art etc. All together with cats as the through theme. We even met a couple of live cats in there!There is also a small museum shop on site.
Quick visit.No possibility to visit the garden currently because is under construction.Friendly and smiling staff
Small museum but SO lovely for any cat lovers !! The staff are friendly and it is just such an enjoyable experience. There is a cute little shop with loads of cat memorabilia - so cute!

This is a great, quirky place to visit. We got to see two of the house cats and love all of the memorabilia and artwork in the house. It doesn't take too long to visit but worth it just to see the cats.
It's a small museum but very cute nonetheless. Quite a big variation when it comes to cat themed art pieces. And of course there are a couple cats roaming around which is super fun 🐈‍⬛🐈‍⬛
The door was locked today 11:00 am.  Saw a sign stating they open 12:00 pm.  I returned 1:30 pm. - door still locked.  Website states it opens 10:00am.  Not impressed & not returning.
Basically a  two storey house of cats photos, posters and cats ornaments!  Didn't see any real cats just a litter box and some chickens in the garden!  10mins in and out for €10. Not worth!!
Interesting if you want to see posters/posters/advertising or various objects related to cats. Otherwise go your way you will earn 10€ per person. And we go around quite quickly (15/20 min).
This is a great little museum of all things cats. Lots of art and photography, including a Picasso. And there are a few cats wandering around.

Quirky, off-beat, run by cat lovers.  If you love cats, you'll love this place.  If you don't, you'll feel you have wasted your 10 euros entrance fee.  If it's for you, this is a good place to spend an hour.Located in an old mansion house, on several floors and with a back garden (open in early March), Katten Kabinet is a labour of love for all things cat.  Lots of pictures, many featured cat themed advertisements going back to the late nineteenth century.  Cat themed furniture, cat themed antiques, cat themed ornaments, cat themed items to buy (many being posters or cards of the advertisements), you get the gist.It's also home to a couple of cats; if you're lucky you may see one (totally ignoring all things human, it's nothing personal).Well, we liked it.

The idea is just brilliant.The house is a collection dedicated to cats in all their forms, including works by well-known artists.It's nice.On the other hand, there is a smell of cat urine.We saw a litter box and cat poop next to it.And finally of this enormous residence, you will only be able to visit the first floor.So €10 sounds like a tourist scam!
There are actually only 2 rooms, so you can get there in less than 5 minutes. So it was actually quite disappointing. The garden is also only open for a very limited period of the year.

Cute little museum with a bunch of cat memorabilia and paintings throughout the years. The back garden isn't open in the winter which was the only bad thing for us! This place has two kitties sleeping around, very good ☺️

Definitely one for cat lovers! This museum is full of cat paintings, statues and many other forms of cat memorabilia. There are also a few live feline friends running around who are absolutely adorable. The staff are friendly and polite. Unfortunately the experience is overpriced at €10 per person. We were in and out within about 25 minutes. Compared with some of the other museums, this doesn't represent great value for money. All bags are removed from you at the door and there are no lockers, they are simply looked after by the staff.

There are exactly three small rooms + a souvenir shop. Very disappointing for 10 euros per person and not recommended. All visitors rush at the cats running around, I'm not surprised that they get scared and run away.

Very much enjoyed this museum. It's very small and super cute, you can do it within 30-45 mins comfortably. They also have actual cats there, the highlight was being able to pet them! The staff were very nice and helpful - they gave us treats to feed the cats! Well worth a visit if you love cats.

I love cats, if you love cats then this is a fun place to visit. If you are lucky you might also see and meet one of the cats that live there. Clearly run by cat lovers, the furniture, posters, pictures and decor are every dog lover and cat hater's nightmare. My visit lasted about 30 minutes, but I did leave smiling.

Lots of art and artefacts for cat lovers and there were even 3 real life cats I spotted during the visit- though you are warned to pet the cats at your own risk! The garden was open and it was a nice place to sit in the fresh air and look at some vintage cat based advertising posters from all over Europe. The visit won’t take long, but if you like cats it’s definitely worth the trip. There is also a shop with numerous cat postcards and artworks.
One of those quirky fun mini-museums that makes Amsterdam so fun. Lots of art and old advertisements featuring cats hosted in a beautiful old home. Really fun!

The kattenkabinet is such a cute little place with some really cool cat pieces that make you smile! It’s only very small but is packed with amazing things and beautiful architecture, the two cats that we saw were extremely friendly and so sweet to have a little cuddle with! Would highly recommend if you have a spare half an hour, love cats, and want to do something that is out of the usual tourist traps :)

We only saw 2 cats.  There is so much potential here, but it isn’t being well-utilized.  One cat was a little friendly and the other was clearly not interested.  A few orange tabbies and this place could be booming with happy cat-loving customers.  We miss our home cats and this did not give us an opportunity to love on the local cats.
If you like cats, go there. So much fun to see. Also you get a got look how an old Amsterdam house is looking inside.
More cats needed. Only three cats were seen and two chickens that seemed to be more friendly than cats

Three rooms with photos, statues and paintings of cats or people with cats. Some quotes about cats. A small garden with some seating. A shop selling posters and postcards of the artwork. Supposedly there are cats here, but we only saw 2. One was in a private area and the other was completely disinterested in us and ran off. Not really a place to go if you want to actually meet some cats, more if you just want to see some weird art vaguely related to cats (some of it was adverts that happened to include a cat).Honestly, not worth the €10 entry fee. Also you have to check your bags which is fine, but they just sit in the entry hall for anyone to steal so not super comfortable with that. If you like a weird museum, then it's maybe worth a visit, otherwise don't bother.

Actually I haven’t even gone yet. I went to the website and purchased a single adult ticket. The company that handles the ticket purchases hasn’t sent a ticket to my email yet. The amount has posted to my Visa so it’s been paid. I contacted them. They asked for a picture. I sent them a picture of the ‘Thank you for your order’ page, and one of the amount posted to my Visa. So far..no response.  Am I too far out for ordering a ticket? My visit isn’t until August.  I bought the ticket 3 days ago.  How long did others wait for their email ticket?  Thank you.

I love cats and had to visit this obscure museum.  You will see posters, paintings, sculptures and photographs of persons that love cats.  Was happily surprised to walk around and see living cats amongst the treasures.  Only go if you love cats!

Enjoyed being surrounded by all things CAT!  It's not a big or fancy place, but it's filled with some terrific pieces.  Thoroughly enjoyed it.  And loved the resident cats!  Gave me a much needed cat fix while missing my own.  (But don't pet the black one anywhere except the top of his head!)  ;)

A fun concept with lots of potential, but ultimately disappointing.  There were plenty of cat-related items and pictures and only minimal information.  For example, two walls of photos of famous people and their cats, but no identifying labels.  OK for a short visit, but not worth the cost of admission in my opinion.

Loved the cat museum! Had an enjoyable visit with my 10 year old boy. Only thing I will say is, there were litter trays around as expected with cats! However in one of the rooms there was a poop smell lingering for the entire hour they we were there. I feel like this is something that could be better monitored by staff.

This was a funny little place with a ton of pictures of famous people with cats, and famous quotes about cats. There was also one cat wandering around that you could pet. Other than that...there was nothing much to it. I expected more information about cats in general, and the garden outside was closed. I had a much more enjoyable time at other museums that were worth way less. I suggest discounting the price if the garden area is closed.
Cute museum filled with cats! Worth a visit. Quirky, unexpected find while visiting Amsterdam, highly recommend spending an hour in here!

Not a true cat lover if you don’t visit! I was visiting Amsterdam for a weekend and had to come to the Cat museum. It’s not the biggest of places but it’s for a true cat lover ❤️ as we came in we were approached by a lovely feline who made her way upstairs (like she was showing us the way).It was a lovely experience and to see that the cats are well looked after makes me really happy ❤️ see you soon kitties

As a cat lover I was really looking forward to visiting this museum and I was not disappointed.  The location was better than expected, being in a canal house on a lovely canal and very close to the Willet-Holthuysen Museum which we visited on the same day.  Yes, they have two cats roaming the rooms who will let you pet them.  It is a small museum and doesn't take a lot of time, but I thought it was well worth the price of admission. Their shop has many posters and postcards of the featured pieces.

If you love cats and have a half an hour spare then I do recommend going.. it’s a very small museum with some interesting pieces, however it is a little overpriced for what is there (they do have a garden but this was closed due to the weather). The highlight of the visit are 2 cats who were laying around which you could fuss.

Lots of adverts and art work involving or about cats, nothing more, it was cheap (ish) entry and 3 average sized rooms full of art.It's a decent way to pass half an hour, but I could only suggest going if you have a few extra euros left towards the end of your trip and time spare.I love cats, and wanted to enjoy this place a lot more, it's fine, nothing amazing and a very short visit over all.

Perfect for cat lovers! My partner was in their element. A bit of strange energy in the fact it’s very old fashioned, a tad creepy but still fun. Can see real life cats also which is great! A little gift shop too that is nice. Only 5€ for students so very inexpensive!

Cute but small museum with a tiny giftshop woth very fun postcards and posters. I bought a poster to take home. It comes in a hard container so it doesn’t get ruined in the plane. I recommend this place if you like cats and small museums, but don’t expect anything too fancy cause this is a very small museum.

Pictures of cats, posters of cats, paintings of cats, even a mummified cat, you get the idea. A nice niche gallery and worth a visit if you like cats. Half price entry with a Go City pass, otherwise it feels a bit expensive.

The collection of this museum is on one floor of a townhouse on a canal. The ground floor hosts a gift shop and the entrance, the floors above are the home of the owner. The rooms are home to four living cats, including one that we met who loved pets and purred. The rest is a collection of cat themed art - posters, sculptures, paintings and a mummified actual cat from ancient Egypt.If you like cats, or whimsical short visits, you will love this place. If you do not, you won't. In our case, the kid loved it, especially the hidden cat art pieces, and most of all the actual cat.
Totally underwhelmed. Love cats but think this was money not well spent. Sorry. It's just on one floor. Some pictures, some statues.

I read other reviews for this museum before I went and they helped me to set appropriate expectations for the experience. I think if you consider this a novelty museum instead of being similar to the Rijksmuseum then that will help you decide if it's right for you to visit.If you're on a budget, don't have a lot of time, or don't really like cats you might want to skip this one. It's cute if you like cats, but it only took me about 20 minutes to walk around it (including the outside area), so the 10€ entry price felt a little steep.

This is a very popular museum due to its fame on social media, and because of staff vacations, it was only open two days a week when we visited. The queue was out the door, but moved quickly. The lone member of staff, who was selling tickets and also handling sales from the gift shop, was friendly and welcoming. There are several rooms of art and cat-related quotes, a lovely little garden decorated with large vintage cat-related advertising, and a little gift shop with posters, postcards and other cat-related items at very reasonable prices. Plus cats! We saw one very friendly cat when we visited, but the others were probably taking shelter elsewhere from the summer heat. The museum costs £10 for an adult ticket, £5 for students and took us about 45 minutes for a thorough visit, including the gift shop. We felt it was good value for money and would recommend it to anyone interested in cats. It’s also a great chance to peek behind the doors of one of Amsterdam’s historic canal houses.

Right in the center of crowded and hectic Amsterdam, there is this little cat lovers’ gem. The museum is not too big, but the items are nice and the garden is a real treat. You could argue about the price being too high, but frankly 10 euro/adult is the price of 1 cappuccino or 5 minutes parking in the city.If you are into cats (arty ones and real ones), then make sure to stop by for 30-45 minutes of joy. If you are into dogs, go find something else…

I love Cats, so it was the place to be in Amsterdam. Only one cat but he was so cute and so happy to be there. To see so many advertising posters with cats, it's crazy, I couldn't resist taking one, I love it! And card games and dollars...

We expected much much more, but it was quite fun to see certain cartoons, paintings and drawings. Of course the cats were a great sight too. Staff is not unfriendly, unlike some others say here. The woman behind the counter is just very... rigid...10 euros for a visit here is too much in our opinion, half the price would have been sufficient (the cats need to be fed eventually!). Overpriced, but still worth a visit if they ever get to the right value for money. We were outside within 30 minutes.
Beautiful, ornate gallery, wonderful art, posters and paintings..staff were lovely, and even kitties on duty!

€10 entry per person for 3 rooms with a couple of cartoonist and abstract style cat posters framed and some cat ornaments/sculptures.  Took us 10 minutes and we went round twice. I have more in my own home and the woman at reception  is incredibly cold.  Probably because she knows this is a rip off and is thinking I'm an idiot for paying in and in fairness, I thought the same. Avoid.

"Not for Children" WORST museum thanks to rude, anti-child staff. My son needed the bathroom and immediately asked when we entered. "Hello, good afternoon" she coldly and sarcastically said looking at my innocent, 7 year old son very rudely. She yelled at him to lift the seat in the bathroom and reprimanded my 10 year old not to touch her tacky fake USD with cats for sale with "wet hands" despite the fact that his hands were not wet. When we left she said "THIS IS NOT REALLY A MUSEUM FOR CHILDREN"😪😰. A museum about cats not for kids? It's a sex or drug museum? I said "that's clear" and "that should be on the website". She is the only really impudent, rude and stupid person that we have met in the Netherlands.

The hotel is easily found and they are super chill with getting there a little later than the time slot you paid for. Super nice receptionist. The hotel itself has many cute cat memorabilia like paintings, sculptures and drawings of cats. But also the architecture and interior design itself are worth mentioning. You feel like you have been transported throughout time into the home of a king or queen who loves cats. The beautiful floor, carpets, walls, wallpapers and chandeliers feel so elegant and authentic, combined with the view of the canals, it’s truly an amazing atmosphere. But the crowning jewels are the two cute cats that wonder around and occasionally accompany you to the next room. The black and white cat is really chill and cute and likes to sleep on the couch. The white one follows you around and rubs up against your legs, really adorable. Please visit this museum, it’s fun, cute and beautiful.

Had booked 2 tickets online as we (myself and my mother) were spending 2 days in Amsterdam and thought we could visit this museum before going back home.  We were greatly dissapointed. Not only did we arrive at the museum to find it closed, there were multiple people there that had also pre booked and left equally dissapointed.  As they only opened at 12 pm we also wasted our morning waiting around until we could go there, then wasted a good hour waiting to see if they would open, answer their phone/doorbell or show any sign of life at all other than the cat that came to investigate.Furthermore I sent them an email requesting a refund of my ticket, and have yet to hear back from them.I would love to be able to give them a positive review but that is impossible if they won't even open on the day that they accept bookings.

Honestly don’t waste your money , went with a huge car enthusiast but was greatly disappointed , unless you love unusual feline art like sculptures or photographs and quotes of famous people’s thought on cats don’t go , expected from the reviews to the museum to be filled with cats , I saw 3 , no in the gift shop , and the outdoor museum wasn’t even open ... better ways of spending an afternoon and €10

Curious little exhibition dedicated to man's furry, enigmatic companion, the cat. The collection is laid out on a single floor, a few rooms stuffed with cat related memorabilia and curiosa. The entrance fee of 7.50 EUR I found a bit stiff given the amount of experience, I mean it's just a lot of cat stuff heaped together. If you're a cat fanatic I suppose it's worthwhile. I did take some pleasure in observing a piece by Hildo Krop, the famous Amsterdam City sculptor, featuring amorous cats. For the rest, meh.

The tickets are pricey considering how little there is to see. There are literally 3 rooms filled with mainly cat sculptures and photos of famous people with cats. Also there are real cats roaming around the place. It says it takes 30 minutes to visit, but we were done after 20. Not sure what else to say.
As avid cat lovers we couldn't skip this place...  It full of cats paintings, sculptures and ads. The bonus is several real live cats that walk in the house and agree to be touched :-)

A good visit for cat lovers, the entry is €10 for adults and €5 for students which I think is quite overpriced. The place is filled with cats, some friendly, some unfriendly. The museum is very small with only about four rooms filled with artwork and does not take long to walk around. The museum is quite tucked away and not a massive tourist destination but worth a visit if you’re a cat lover!

I love cats and had been missing my own while travelling when i stumbled across this place. Easily my favourite place in Amsterdam. I absolutely adore all of the odd sculptures and beautiful art; and the 5 cats that roam the place. Plan to visit again real soon!
If you love cats, this place is for you. It is a small museum but was a perfect way to start our trip after day of traveling. There are cats you can pet, which helped to de-stress everyone.

We enjoyed our visit here, even if it was a little pricey. I love cats and there was some beautiful artwork on display. It was lovely to peruse the collection. There were four cats in the museum, one was parricularly friendly and fussy! The garden was open but as it was heavily raining, we just looked through the windows. The little souvenir shop was cute, I bought some postcards home with me. A nice find!

This was only 5 euros with an iamsterdam card and I wouldn't have been happy paying full price.  I'm the biggest cat fan (hence the username) but the museum wasn't as big as I anticipated.  We did meet a gorgeous friendly little boy called Billy, though, just as we were about to leave.  It was lovely to be able to pet a cat while away from home and missing our own.   I think the museum is missing a trick by not marketing itself as a cat cafe - it is a beautiful house with beautiful rooms and would probably do very well with all the cat lovers who regularly visit.
This was a delightful chance to see cats and cat-related art (including a special exhibit) in a gorgeous Golden Bend townhouse.

As a massive car lover I really enjoyed this museum, I really loved seeing the 2 cats wondering around and sleeping. One was more friendly than the other. The only downfall in my opinion was the price, $7 for 3 small rooms and the garden was not accessible.

If you have cats or have a bit of time to spare, you'll enjoy this whimsical museum. You don't have to be a 'cat lady' to go. The Katten Kabinet is a bit tongue-in-cheek, witness: a book called "My cat. My soulmate." It's got some really old signs etc... and some unique objects. Beautiful artwork on the ceiling and an amazing mirror. It is quite small-my visit may have been 30 minutes. There was a real cat lying on the counter where I bought my ticket. It is near Blomenmarket.

As per the title says, it did not contain a lot of things. But the main reason we entered was because of the cats. And it was nice meeting cats while away from home, if that is your purpose than i really recommend, but you can not be certain that you will meet a cat

A beautiful little place for cat lovers with it's own furry feline resident who is very friendly.  Beautiful pictures and objects and 2 beautiful upstairs rooms. Very relaxing and calming.Enjoyed the gift shop. Many poster versions of the art on display.  Reasonably priced.

Amongst the high energy of the city centre, I loved visiting the Cat Cabinet. Such a lovely and calm place where you can view many pieces of cat art, photos and musings about cats. Stroking the real cats is also a bonus. Small but perfectly formed museum.

Before you go read up on the history of this museum. It is heartwarming and shares insights into why the collection exists. This is an excellent gallery especially for lovers of cats and art. It is also a beautiful example of an old Dutch canal house. I have noticed some reviews complaining of the size of the museum and the entry cost. This is not the Rijksmuseum and you shouldn't go in expecting to spend several hours wandering around halls with vaulted ceilings. Take this delight at face value. It may be a small collection but it's a good one. Read all of the texts, soak it all in, and you'll have a great time.

Even though the visit doesn’t last long (one floor only), it worths it for all cats lovers. You’ll discover objects and paintings about cats in a beautiful bourgeois house. You may have the chance to see cats wandering around. I was alone and enjoyed each part. It’s always mind blowing to be able to enter such a bourgeois atypical house and have the chance to discover an ancient century. We should all not take it for granted and appreciate it.

Visited this small but wonderfully intimate museum where you have close access to artwork featuring Cats. The setting is wonderful in a private home on the Golden Bend and just as inspiring as the artwork. With lots to see and read in the three rooms we took far longer than 15 minutes stated in some reviews and well worth the entrance fee.
This is a nice attraction but not necessary. It is very small and takes about 15 minutes to see everything. Very cute cats going around (we saw 4).

Mixed feelings. Very unusual, perhaps unique, exhibition of cat art, though quite small and easily all seen in under an hour.  Overheard people behind us muttering, "is that it for 7 Euros?" and we could sympathise to a degree, though it brought us joy - especially the live cats wandering the house - and that must be the mark of a good exhibition.  Many exhibits, like the cat painted on the Austin Mini(?) bonnet, could do with a line or two of explanation.  The garden was closed off without explanation.  Use of their WC was a bonus in Amsterdam, and stroking the two kittens made my wife's day, so that gained a star.

Ok... its a museum ... for cats. About cats. For people about cats. Everything in it, has... a cat. and best of all ... YOU CAN PET THE EXHIBITS. Listen ... we made a video about it ... its meowerful stuff. link in the bio... you will be charmed.

My travelling companion loves all things cats so this was a must see for her. I'm allergic but fortunately there was only one live one around - although it was so perfectly still we both thought it stuffed at first!An interesting place. One, you get to see inside a canal house and can get a feel for how people live here. Two, you get to see loads and loads of cat related art, posters and objects. There was also a short animated video dubbed into English all about the Cat who walked by Himself that we were a bit mesmerised by. Low key but fun.
The whole thing took about 10 minutes, expensive price and no real cats (1 sleeping at the entrance)

This is a small but eclectic museum filled with cat memorabilia. It is obviously eccentric but beautifully arranged and lovingly preserved. We were privileged to meet three feline occupants. A must visit for anyone who loves cats.
Really lovely place to visit, and there were a few cats running around too :) however the price was high considering it was only a few small rooms and corridors. I would still recommend it, though!
All things cats, paintings and art, posters and the best, cats real ones throughout the museum...it was as if the cats ran the place
Few cats, few paintings of cats. Nothing more, nothing less. Interesting if you want to see a building from canal ring from the inside.
Its a lovely place to amble around for an hour. Meet Billy, he is ultra cool. The rest is an homage to all things feline.

This includes URLs / web site addresses / links to other web sites.If there is a web site you would like to share with our travel community, please do so in the Travel Forum section of our site. TripAdvisor Support Team ________________________________________The Cat Cabinet Amsterdam Unique VR film experience in garden of Kattenkabinet 688614145 If you happen to be in Amsterdam the week of 13 thru 19 July, buy tickets to this individual VR film  in the garden of the Kattenkabinet.= 20 moving minutes and more than worth the 8 euro price. You will find it online as Ahorse.And check this link other times as well. It doesn't play all the time but you might get lucky that it's showing when you're visiting.And on the way out visit the lovely canal house rooms of the museum and its amazing museum shop. An absolute must for cat lovers!
The place is strangely worth a visit but the fee a little to high. But the posters and rooms are cool. Beware of some of the cats, they bite us!

This beautiful canal house that was built in 1667, for two of the van Loon brothers, is a must see when you are in Amsterdam, even if you are not a cat lover!  The museum was founded in 1990 by a wealthy dutchman who wanted to preserve the memory of his beloved cat.  As a cat lover, I can understand why!  The museum is full of sculptures, paintings, photos and other art devoted to cats.  I visited with my husband and young daughter (also a cat lover) and they loved it.  The cost is only six euros for adults and 3 euros for children.  It is an interactive experience as you might be lucky enough to see and pat some of the resident cats.  The gift shop was great too, and we picked up some cool posters and trinkets to remember the experience.  If you are looking for something a bit off beat and you love cats (and gorgeous canal houses) this is a must.

The building is rather ordinary on the outside but inside the decor and furnishings are exquisite, crystal chandeliers and ceiling paintings. We had half price admission with coupon from De Poezenboot. Nice display of cat themed art along with snoozing resident felines.

I had some reservations about visiting a cat museum, but it turned out to be a beautiful, quirky little house that's ideal for cat lovers.  It has an impressive collection of drawings, paintings, sculptures, and posters of cats, some by quite well-known artists such as Rembrandt and Picasso.  There is also a room of photographs of famous people with their cats.  The whole thing is presided over by the resident live cats, who are quite gracious in tolerating the constant flow of tourists through their domain!  The human staff was very friendly and helpful as well.

Unexpected find, only really for cat lovers. Very unique old house with cat art. There were also actual cats as you can see from the photos. Not a big museum, but worth seeing if you are a cat lover to support the place.

This place is ideal for cat lovers. There is a quirky collection of cat stuff and a lot of posters and paintings dedicated to the feline universe. When I have visited there was an interesting photography exhibition about famous people and their furry friends. There are also four cat residents which are sweets and love to be cuddled by visitors. Highly recommended for people who are really interested on these amazing creatures.

We are cat lovers but were also intrigued by the reputation of the house...and not disappointed. This is a beautifully restored and maintained house with some exceptional “ceiling jewelry” as well as lovely art and curiosities such as photos of celebrities with their cats and memorabilia related to famous cats and cat owners. I would have loved to have learned more about how the museum came to be how it is maintained from a docent but that did not seem available. There appeared to be an interesting back yard too but that was off limits. You get a discounted admission with the Iamsterdam card and the gift shop has some absolutely terrific posters.

Perfect for a cat lover on a rainy day or if you're at a loose end but not really a destination museum. A little smaller than I expected and sadly found none of the resident felines wandering around on the day I visited. Enjoyed the current photo exhibition of cats and artists. Your visit will take about 30 minutes as it's only three rooms and a gift shop.
Small museum, with a collection of all things cat. Plus a few resident cats who live there . Museum has a good shop with lots of cat posters for sale. And yes as a cat lover I’m a bit bias !

Small museum with cats-oriented art collection. It's a little too dark in some of the rooms, so sometimes difficult to see the details. Few real cats walking and sleeping in the museum. Overall- worth a vizit only if you like cats. Nice neighborhood, lots of shops and restaurants.
The collection is rather small, but eclectic. The bonus is that there are three live kittie residents foot those travelers missing their companions from home.

You don't have to be a cat lover to enjoy this quirky little museum, but it helps if you are :)Only saw two kitties while we were there and they looked like they owned the place. Enjoyed the art and the home. Creaky wooden floors galore. Fun spot. Won't take you long to run through it.

This little gem lies alongside the Herengracht. I planned on visiting because I'm a cat lover myself. The museum features artwork (paintings, posters, statues, pictures, ...) with cats (duh :) ). There are also some real live cats hanging around! And they might just want to be petted!

This museum is small but full of personality, in a grand house by a canal with tall windows, high-ceilinged rooms and collection of sculptures, posters, paintings and ephemera celebrating cats. The laid-back cats-in-residence are just the icing on the cake. In nice weather, the garden in the back is a good place to rest your feet and look at a display of more cat-oriented advertising posters. One poignant touch indoors -- a desk where visitors leave notes about their impressions, and in many cases pay tribute to their own cat companions, past and present. At 7 euros admission for adults, it was certainly worth a stop.

It was surprising to find art pieces from grand names like Picasso and Rembrandt, and a piece from the Cats musical. However, it is a rather small museum, and it seems like there are rooms not being utilized for the museums purpose? This might be why the price is very good, but I cannot help but feel like more could be done. It is a perfect place for a cafe! Also surprised by the gift shop, which did not have as many novel pieces as hoped. Feels like more could be done here. It was nice to see the cats, when they felt like it!

We really enjoyed this quirky, cluttered and interesting museum. It's a great collection of, well, cat stuff! Paintings, advertising, graphic design, ornaments etc. Well worth an hour of your time and some nice things in the gift shop.

Came here out of interest from reading previous reviews here on TripAdvisor. It was €7 which is hardly breaking the bank and it was in a beautiful old house opposite the canal. The museum was filled with interesting cat art work and sculptures and there were even a few extremely friendly real cats roaming around the house which was lovely. A very unique little experience that is ideal for cat lovers.

This is a small exhibition rather than a museum. It is full of pictures and sculptures of cats with some very quirky pieces. There were 5 cats wandering around, al friendly and will to have their ears tickled.

I would recommend as something to do if you are a cat lover in Amsterdam. There doesn't really seem to be much order to things and I was surprised that it was as small as it was (we were only there for around 30 minutes and were able to see everything fully). However, despite this I was pleased to go and meet some of the resident cats, the staff were pleasant and with an Iamsterdam card cost €5 each which I felt was reasonable. When we went it was very quiet and relaxed and we were able to walk around at our leisure.

This was an interesting diversion, a grand canal house on the Golden Bend that is full of cat art (and cats) collected by a cat-obsessed rich guy. I'm not sure it was worth the price of admission, though it was fun to see inside one of those fancy houses. The kids really enjoyed trying to find and pet all the resident cats, too.

We really hoped that more would be on offer at this museum. My husband actually believes it’s someone’s house and she opens a few rooms to exhibits to cover costs... that’s how poor it was! There was literally 3 rooms and a hallway with a few pictures, ornaments and posters and two cats wondering about. To be honest, I have a more impressive collection at my house with Tom’s Drag and other portraits I have acquired over time. Really not worth €7 each. Left feeling quite cheated and deflated.

It's just wonderful to see real cats walking around the area. You could also pet some. Art is pretty, but my main attention went to the real cats. The staff was very friendly. For students 4eur per ticket, I think its good value. Nice place to spend like 40min-1h. Not very much to see.

This is an art museum that has exclusively works with cats in them.If you don't like cats there is nothing for you here!I like cats so spent a very enjoyable 45 minutes looking round several times, it's absolutely jam packed with paintings, curios, sculptures and posters. There may even be a live cat or two who relish attention!Situated on a side road next to a canal, you need to search the place out which is the only negative. It's worth finding though.

Mostly this museum is hilarious- they could charge twice the amount and I’d still be happy - but there are also some art gems from Picasso, historical oddities from Lenin and advertising treasures hidden in the two floors which make this a must visit.

Came across this quirky themed house in the Golden Band area, after our canal tour guide mentioned it. It’s €7 to enter and it’s an upstairs floor filled with cat photos, paintings, pictures and statues of our feline friends! AND, there are cats wondering around. Brilliant! We saw four chilling around the house, including one napping in the little shop area. Might seem a little pricey for one floor but getting to view the interior of the building was a bonus with its stunning period features and beautiful decor. Would recommend visiting this musuem

An interesting and certainly different exhibition for cat friends. A mix of posters, sculptures, artwork, dishes and even live cats in a beautiful old flat with garden. There is also a shop selling cards and posters, which you can visit without buying a ticket to exhibition.

Such a cute museum, and the resident cats are so sweet! Great way to spend a rainy afternoon in the city, or any type of afternoon. Some real gems here too, pieces by Picasso and Rembrandt to name a few
A fun little cat museum in central Amsterdam. Recommended for crazy cat people everywhere. Interesting old house,which is a nice bonus.

Unmissable if you adore cats . The story of this home is part of its beauty . It’s not just some museum . Really stunning . Look up see the ceilings ! Stunning art and one of the few places in an overcrowded city we felt relaxed . The people are nice too . Go early to avoid too many people and get good video . The cats are there you just need to look . Often they will find you . The city is only nice for this a the Cat boat . The canal tours are pricey and touristy . Not a great deal learned on those tours . Best to read ahead . An amazing facade on Gentleman’s canal . Some of the very few nice people of very little we had experienced in the city . Locals seem to dislike visitors immensely
a delightful place to spend time with an eclectic collection of feline memorabilia, rooms and rooms of posters, paintings, dinnerware, statues.

This is a small art museum with various cat related pieces of art and 4 real live cats who wander the place as well. The art is all on one floor of this old house and it only takes about 20-30 minutes to see it all. It was nice for me because I love cats and spent a good 15 minutes just cuddling with one of the cats but my friend was pretty underwhelmed. The gift shop is also pretty disappointing. None of the post cards have Kattenkabinet on them and there weren't any cute catsy shirts or sweatshirts for sale. Overall, I probably won't visit ever again but I don't really regret going.

It was interesting to walk through the rooms where the cat art was displayed. The focus was on historical art and a couple cat artists from the past. It takes about 30 minutes to see everything, for 7 euros.  I believe they could expand and liven up the place with some more modern, and eclectic artists as well. I was also really hoping to get a fun, cat-oriented t-shirt, but there was only one kind in the gift shop and it was not what I wanted. I assume they could have a local artist design/screenprint something on tshirts for them and it would not be too expensive. This would have been nice rather than the overload of vintage posters and postcards - especially since I assume mostly tourists come there, and only once. It was a pleasure to be visited by one of the friendly kitties who apparently are resident at the museum.

If you're a cat lover, drop by this small museum of cat-inspired art. You won't need long, as it's just a few rooms, but there are four adorable cats that call the museum home that love a good scratch behind the ears. Something very unique and off the beaten track if you're in Amsterdam. If you're just going to complain about it not being somewhere you'll spend a lot of time, give it a miss.

We like cats....nevertheless we had lowish expectations....which were not met. We were in and out in 5 minutes. In fairness the artwork was properly and carefully curated but it just didn't tick any boxes for us. Maybe we're not sufficiently cat mad.

Near the city centre, interesting place but very small. €7 for entry which seems a bit overpriced, cool cat art and a few cats that run around the place. There was a litter tray in part of the museum though that absolutely stunk making it less enjoyable to see that section. Interesting place to see but expected a bit more.

As you walk in you are greeted by a warm welcome from reception and maybe some cats then you step upstairs into some quirky rooms with pictures, posters, paintings, drawings or statues and frames of different kinds of cats and backgrounds. If you’re especially fortunate then you may be greeted by a few breeds of cats you are allowed to stroke them and pet them like your own then you go back downstairs into a wide selection of different cat quirky things in the gift shop and on the way out you may find a little furry friend or two to stroke. (Written by Freya aged 10) who loved the place!

Quite small but full of nice cat art. Also great to see the inside of one of the old townhouses, if you’re not so into cats! Friendly staff and nice reasonably priced shop with books, T-shirts and fab posters

This is a small quirky museum with cats portrayed in various art mediums.  There were also 2 resident feline greeters who were happy to provide a quick kitty-fix for those of us missing our feline friends.  It also provides a glimpse of the interior of a traditional Amsterdam row house.
Very new experience for me and I would highly recommend it for Amsterdam visitors special for cats lovers. Something interesting is waiting for you
This was a really cute museum. Huge collection of all things cat related and reasonably priced entrance fee. Perfect for a crazy cat lady :)
Such a quirky museum and well worth the entrance fee to see the interesting artwork and posters plus we got to fuss the cats

This place took a little bit of finding but worth it. The owners have collected a vast amount of cat memorabilia and it is displayed over three rooms. Pictures, ornaments, and there are even a few felines strutting about like they own the place - which they obviously do!It was a shame you could not get out into the back garden as there was much more to see there.

The ticket was a little much for what this is (in my opinion) but I really enjoyed it. It's a floor of a canal house completely full of cat art. The back yard has more art, and I got a visit from one of the house cats. He didn't want to be pursued, but I sat down and showed a little interest and he came for some attention. I enjoy some odd attractions, and this was perfect. If you're a fan of cats, you will likely have a nice visit.
My wife loves cats so we gave it a try. Mostly advertising posters. Only one cat in lobby, purr.Garden not open.

Found this place by accident, but it was all I ever wanted in a museum. I love cats and I would recommend you go here if you have the obsession for cats that I do. If you’re not a cat-lover this place may turn you... beware. I met one of the resident cats Billy, they were very sleepy and we didn’t manage to meet the other cats who live there. The lady on the reception was pleasant and helpful. The gift shop is amazing if you’re looking for decor or a quirky present for a cat-lover.
Small novelty museum, but for what it is they do it well. My toddler especially liked the real cats.

My mother and I visited the KattenKabinet, and, as cat lovers, it was quite a charming museum for us. The house was old-fashioned, artistic, and very pleasant to go through. A quaint garden in the back was available for visitors to walk through as well. We found many nice gifts in the gift shop, and the location along one of Amsterdam's premier canals-the Herengracht-placed it near other attractions as well. Very, very nice!
Situated on the side of the Herengracht. This quirky little museum is must for all feline lovers. Gift shop has plenty of souvenirs and is highly recommended.

Like most people reviewing here, we love cats. This is not really a museum, more 'someones lifetime collection of cat themed art.' Not sure if other parts are sometimes open, but all we got was one floor of a house, definitely not worth the price. You could see there was more stuff going upstairs (I know the person lives there, it looked like 2 more floors though), and in the garden, both places were out of bounds though.The website says they have 4 cats, we saw 3, and petted 2. Interesting enough collection of stuff.Think that €10 for a family of 4 would be a fairer price, and get rid of the sourpuss lady at the door.

As a art&cat lover this was a nice place to visit. There's a lot of vintage-styled posters f. ex. old advertisement posters with cats. There's also things to see in the garden. There were two cats, one of them came to welcome me at the entrance. As a student I got to go here for 4€, but i think the full price of 7€ is quite high because the tour is quick.

A quirky small museum inside a neat old house, it is dedicated to art featuring cats. It includes more than just paintings, incorporating memorabilia like a costume from the musical "Cats". My kids loved it and it was a great quick and unusual stop in our visit to Amsterdam.

I LOVE cats and I'm speedily on my way to becoming a crazy cat lady like the one who owns this "museum." I use the term loosely because this is basically a weird old lady's stinky house that's filled with cat  paraphernalia and a few random felines. Oh, and she charges 10 bucks to get inside.The whole place reeks of cat urine, but I only saw two live kitties. There's a bunch of disturbing cat-themed "artworks" and tapestries and crap piled high in every corner. Playbills, photographs, varying degrees of awfulness in the form of sculpture and paintings, and photocopied images of cats stuck in frames. I am not making this up.I know, I know, it sounds amazeballs AWESOME. Sadly, it's not. I wanted to love this place but I couldn't get out of there fast enough. I walked way out of my way to visit and I left sorely disappointed. This cat lover is pawsitively (hee!) stunned at the high rating of this tourist trap and rip-off.My husband summed it up perfectly when he turned to me and said "why the heck did we PAY to see this, we have cats at home!" Even the small gift shop was a huge letdown.My advice is to save your dough for something worthwhile in Amsterdam and just go find the weird cat lady in your own hometown and invite yourself over for tea. Same experience for sure.

It's in a semi-basement leading up to 2 floors so not a huge museum but still fun to visit, especially for cat lovers. If I'm not mistaken the owner still lives upstairs and sometimes comes down? (or was this years and years ago)
Very happy we found this!  A must see for cat lovers!  Very quaint. Seems very “ off the beaten path”! Gift shop is awesome

It was a quirky menagerie of feline finds from mummified cats to fine art to advertisements using cats.  We got to see two of the house cats that reside there, one which even acted as tour guide and led us up to the one floor museum.  The garden has an exhibit in it too.  Some of the pieces are housed in broken glass which makes me wonder why they are out on display and there are some that are just faded and worn out.  There is a little shop with magnets, bags, cards and posters all at a reasonable price too. It took my daughter and I just over half an hour to look at the exhibit.  This man definitely loves cats!  It was a fun experience overall for us.

A museum of cats!! Pictures, ornaments, furniture etc all featuring cats. Billy Willy and Oscar the housecats roaming about, sleeping on tables, posing on chairs and generally being cute. A fun way to spend an hour

The museum has some interesting cat art. The gift shop is small, but cute. The garden was closed, even though it was a beautiful day. Didn't see a single live cat and the lady at the front was not overly friendly or helpful, she almost seemed annoyed. Overall, not sure it was worth the 7 Euros. You could pop in the gift shop and that would be sufficient.

We spotted this place on our Blue Boat Canal Tour. We decided if we were in the area we would check it out. On a walk the next day we came pretty close so we stopped in. The price was a little high and we did not have much time, so we opted to just visit the gift shop on the street level. We were able to pick something up for our cat sitter and decided that would be enough.

I visited on my own in June 2018, and enjoyed it, although it was quite expensive for entry, and one can't realistically spend more than, say, an hour here, without some special reason. It does include a great many cat-related objects, paintings, posters, etc., many of which are worth seeing. It also houses an actual live cat, who I didn't get to interact with because s/he was monopolised by other visitors!

This is definitely one of the weirdest places I’ve ever been. Its basically someone’s collection of a few cat items that clearly got out of hand- but I love it.Its about 3 rooms totally full of cat themed items. Even the garden is full of them. The owner still lives upstairs with this family. The gift shop has a nice selection of posters, magnets and various cat themed materials.Plus, 3 cats live there and roam around freely. It was the highlight of my week.

The museum is a beautiful old home that has a bunch of cat-related stuff in it.  In addition, three nice felines prowl the place and offer some diversion.  We took our time and were done in less than 30 minutes.  Nothing special.

This is basically just someone’s home, filled with cat-themed paraphernalia and posters.  It took us about half an hour to get through the entire place, not worth the entrance fee (unless maybe they donated that to cat shelters, but I’m not sure if they do).  The posters and paintings are cute but there seemed to be a lot of areas that were off-limits.  We met a couple of their cat residents who were happy to give some boops - they were pretty much the highlight of the place.

A thrown together range of cat pictures, bits and pieces, sculptures. The receptionist did not have change and when asked why not said "it was the start of the day" - so why do they not keep some from previous day. Backpacks to be left at reception with no security. Not worth the money

A small cat-themed museum, which we were excited to visit but left us a bit underwhelmed. Entrance costs €7 per person, but sadly we whizzed through the museum in about 10 minutes, as it’s basically just a few grand rooms with various paintings of cats hanging on the walls. I was somehow hoping for more in terms of more descriptions, information & exhibits, description. The best bit was probably the gift shop, which sells various books, as well as some beautiful posters & postcards, & you can pop in to browse for free!

Expected more.....not just a collection of old posters, cat adds from the past, lithography and some sculputures. Gives an impression that someone collected few things connected with cats and put it on a bunch. Few famous people with "cat touch" (Pisacco, Lenin,...). I'm cat crazy but 7 euros entrance fee is not worth it.

We found this hidden gem whilst researching our trip to Amsterdam. It was a few euros to enter and it really was an Aladdins cave of cat art. Very unique experience and worth every cent. We even saw one of the owners cats relaxing on the windowsill (definitely spent a good 10 minutes giving it a good fuss!!)    The house itself is gorgeous, and is a fantastic opportunity to see inside one of the old canal houses!!

The Kattenkabinet is a really cool museum based in an elegant old canal house in Amsterdam. The museum contains examples of cats in culture through the past several centuries, in paintings, posters and many other artifacts. It's a fairly quick but very fun visit. Even if you're not into cats, it might be worth the entrance fee to see the beautiful canal house interior.
This is a unique museum for those who share their lives with cats.  Wonderful to see familiar posters and original paintings in real life and in the beautiful setting of this grand Amsterdam house.
This hidden gem is worth the quick dip in. Filled with more cats than your loneliest single friend. This is a trip, and inexpensive adventure after a coffee shop.
Someone’s old collection of cat paintings.... not quite what I expected. There is a resident cat who cares not for pets. The building is beautiful however.

I am not a cat person but I really liked this place! I was mostly impressed by the house itself which has a few baroque style rooms with friendly cats walking around! I would like more items in the gift shop!
Thought there would be some cats roaming about but just one on the sofa in reception who soon did a runner!

It's a small museum and every surface is covered in cat art.  That's the good part - well, that, and the 3 resident cats lounging about.  But it's really expensive for what you get.  I am a HUGE cat person and I just just disappointed.  Would not recommend.

The idea of a cat museum seemed interesting so decided to give it a ago without researching it. Adult entry was €7 each, which seemed reasonable for a small museum in Amsterdam. The biggest problem with this place is that it is a lot smaller than it looks when you enter. Most of the bottom floor is closed off. You go upstairs to 3 small rooms, the rest are closed off. There’s an outside area, but that’s closed off. There’s an upstairs too, also closed off. The exhibition itself too just seems to be a lot of random pictures of cats with no context. Useful information and interesting facts are nowhere to be found. Feels like being in the home of a cat obsessed maniac, but there are only about two cats wondering about. Saw everything in about 15 minutes and was ready to leave. This experience cost €14 for the both of us.

It’s a great place if you like cats in art! The bonus was the real cat upstairs by the window which really enjoys the attention! Shop was good and reasonably priced. Definitely only for lovers of all things feline!

Ok, it might help a bit if you're a cat lover... However, Kattenkabinet is really a celebration of one man's single-minded accumulation of an amazing collection of items. Lovingly tended and displayed, and focussed around the aristocratic 'JP Morgan' who features in their logo. There are also roaming felines who are happy to sit for a photo or a pat...or maybe just walk away if they feel like it. Totally worth a visit if you're in the neighbourhood.

Full of all kinds of cat imagery and even some real ones! Wish there was a bit more information on some of the pictures though. The building itself is beautiful and it's a great way to spend some time leisurely. The shop is really sweet as well.

I liked this place very much. There were lots of cat related art and one actual cat sleeping. Staff was friendly and ticket was 4€ for student. From shop you could buy cards and books like museums usually sell, lots of great stuff!

What a fantastic way to spend an afternoon! The museum is interesting, fun and heartwarming. The art is wonderful, there is a real family feel to the place, and there are a few cats wandering around purring - heaven! Costs 8€ for an adult ticket, 4€ for concessions. Worth the money just to see Picasso's cat sketch. The gift shop is also adorable - but I wish there were more prints/postcards of the art on display.

The Cat Cabinet combined three of my favourite things - architecture, art and cats. The house was aesthetically pleasing and provided a beautiful backdrop for a wonderful collection if paintings, sketches, posters, statues and even a mummy of a cat. We saw three of the living residents, one who was very friendly and intrigued by visitors. The cats were very much a part of the exhibits and part of the reason we went. It is fantastic to be able to enter this private residence and see such a marvellous collection

Went here with my teenage daughter and she loved it, but I thought it was a bit expensive for what it was.  She spent ages stroking the large black tom cat on display in the window.  A real mix of eclectic cat posters and paintings in large ornate rooms.

OK, I am all about dogs.  Love them can give without them.  But my girl friend is alls cats( I guess opposites  attract).  I found this place amazing.   Tim Berners-Lee wrote the most un-suspected thing about the internet was cats, and now I know he was right.

Nothing more to say really. I visited several galleries and museums on my visit to Amsterdam and found this place the most expensive for what you get in return. Some interesting pieces, and I appreciate these things need to be paid for somehow. But it didn't really have any character to it.

Well, it is just as weird as you imagine. All together it manages to amaze you, even as no big fan of cats. I would go here, just because it is so unlike anything else I have experienced. For better and worse.
Nice surrounding and beautiful rooms for cat art works and three museum cats. Very nice and friendly staff.

I’m not a cat person, but my friend is. This was actually very cool! It was a nice surprise and inexpensive. Definitely crazy cat lady vibes, but made for some photos... that of which, TripAdvisor will not populate along with many other photos.

This is a charming old Amsterdam house (which the owner apparently still lives in) which is full of art, posters, and other paraphernalia related to cats. There's far less of it than I was expecting, but it's a different kind of thing to see than you'll get in most places.If you like cat art, then you'll like it.If you don't, then you probably won't.

My girlfriend is a cat lover. So we went to the Katten kabinet. We were out in about ten minutes. If it wasn't for the 5 minutes she spent cuddling the only real cat in the building, we would have been back on the sidewalk in 5 minutes.So be warned, you better use your 7€ per person somewhere else.

Fortunately I didn’t have to pay the €7.00 entrance fee to the Katten Kabinet, as the art gallery entrance was included on my I Amsterdam card.My visit to the Katten Kabinet was a strange and surreal experience.  Located on the Herengracht, this feline themed art gallery/museum is housed in a couple of upstairs rooms in a large canal house.The “art” was mainly posters with a sprinkling of ornaments and artifacts.  Nothing was well displayed and seemed to have been placed without much care or attention to detail.I’m not really sure who would benefit from visiting this establishment.  I’m a self-confessed cat lover and the only things that appealed to me were the live sleeping cats that inhabit the place.  My visit is best summed up as being quirky and on the edge of being totally bizarre.

Lovely attraction tucked away by the canal. Staff were really friendly, nice gift shop and easily accessible toilet. Cost €7 to enter and there wasn't much to see.. lots of nice cat paintings and we got to stroke a cat, but it was very frustrating to see a garden filled with cat art OUT of the window but closed off to the public. If this was €3 maybe I would've felt happier about it. Finished the attraction in about 10 minutes

We have cats and love them.  While this museum had some cute cat stuff in it half of it was closed off from the public. I saw one cat in a closed off section there. I can only recommend going to this if it cost 1 euro or was free. Possibly the worst tourist attraction I have been to and there was potential for it to be better.

This "museum" is definitely something different! Basically, it is a collection of various things (posters, sculptures, paintings, etc.) with cats on them. It sure is interesting, a little bit odd, but also fun. There are two cats strolling around who don't mind being pet, so that, too, is a plus if you are a cat lover. I would recommend going there if you have some spare time, but do not expect too much. The exhibition is rather small and 7€ seem to be a rather expensive price for a place you can visit within 20 minutes. There is an interesting shop at the end of the tour and the atmosphere is nice.
Fun visit but don’t expect to much of it. It’s a collection of weird and fun stuff that has cats on it. Maybe a bit small (basically one storie of the house) for the price but still fun.

If you love cats, don't miss this museum! The mid century posters are a feast for the eyes if you love design AND cats, too. There are lots of unique pieces and the interior of the building itself is lovely. I really enjoyed it all!

If you love cats and art, this is the place for you! Lots of cat-related artifacts and art in a beautiful old mansion. The best part - kitties actually live there. There are four - we only saw two, but all the guests were eager to take pictures with them!Very fun museum.
Cats, cats, cats.  Very cute, small museum dedicated to cat art, posters, etc.  Some of the best examples of 20th century European cat-related advertising too.  Recommended, if you like cats : )

I have a cat and a medium sized house. From tomorrow, you are welcome to visit for 7 Euros. You can stroke my cat if she puts in an appearance. Similar to this attraction, I will not let you in our garden but I'll offer you a cup of tea, which you can't get here. The decor in the Cat Cabinet IS more ornate and there are more artefacts but that's about it!

Fun quirky place! I really enjoyed seeing the interior of this beautiful home - turned - museum. Its chuck full of interesting cat art, sculpture, and other quirky objects. Bonus- real cats hanging out that you can pet.

The highlight of the place is the real cats lounging at the entrance who seem happy to have the company of visitors. The museum is in a typical Amsterdam building on the canal belt and gives you a glimpse of what these houses look like from inside with their high ceilings and large windows. The price is a bit high for a museum that is quite small but it has an interesting collection of paintings, posters and some sculptures.

The Cat Museum ( Kattekabinet ) is a must for cat lovers, or anybody with an interest in art that is cat - related.It's a small venue with the shop on the ground floor and the museum on the top floor.There is a bit of everything including old posters, statues, paintings and the obligatory live cat passed out on the couch.1980 costume of Griselda from Cats is a nice surprise.Well worth the visit.

Yes, for sure - only go if you love cats! It's quirky, weird, dusty, small and quiet - perfect antidote to all the major museums in the city. Housed in a beautiful, but slightly run-down building - which only adds to it's charm! Resident cats add to the character of the place. I'm so glad I went!

This is a small museum dedicated to "J.P. Morgan", the deceased cat of the museum owner.  As others have mentioned, this venue will be enjoyed most by cat lovers.
