There are paintings, prints, posters, statues, and all manner of cat artwork.  To me, the best part was a small area tucked off the second floor hallway.  This area had some paraphernalia (Louis Vuitton case for his travel litter box), birthday gifts and portraits of "J.P. Morgan"... it is obvious this cat was special to his owner.  This was my favorite part of the whole museum, so if you go be sure to look for it and read the placards.If you like off-the-beaten-path museums, this one is fun.  I found it easy to pop into the Bag & Purse Museum, then into the Kat Kabinett and then onto the Dutch Costume Museum... all right on the Herengracht and can be completed in just a few hours.

If you do want to visit here go to the De Poezenboot first you will then get a 50% discount on the entry price. Annoyingly we didn't realise this until it was too late!We walked from the centre and it took about 40 minutes - nice scenery to look at on the way. Well worth the visit you forget how cats feature in such a lot of things

I love cats, therefore I went to the cat museum. It is very cute, all the pictures made me smile, but it is small you only need 30 mins in there, and seriously if you not a cat lover you won't enjoy it at all.

Not much to see here we spent maybe 5 minutes Walking around only a few rooms open and there was one cat sleeping in one sofa and he did not even care that I tried to pet him/her. Not Worth the Money or the time to go here.
Well, the website said you could spend up to 40 minutes at this venue. 10 minutes at a stretch! This was poor value for 7 euros.I would avoid completely.

My wife and I are both cat lovers, so we decided to check out this museum on a rainy day. It was some posters and artwork on the second floor of a small building. Nothing of interest here. Only spent 15 minutes.

Maybe the most bizarre art gallery that I have ever visited. As advertised it is just cat art and there are cats roaming around. For all its oddness you still may want to see it and my son really liked the cat dollars that we bought in the gift shop.

What appeals so much about this museum is how laid back it is. There's no one bothering you about giving in your luggage and a few cats walk around calmly accepting strokes. One even greeted us at the door!The museum doesn't oversell anything. it is was it says on the tin, it's  nice, quirky relaxed stop off. I also loved being with fellow cat lovers :)

In the Kattenkabinet you'll find cats as sculptures, in paintings and in posters, and also walking around the house and in the garden. If you are into cats you'll like this place.The shop has got postcards, posters and books for sale.The children of the house ran a delightful little cafe in the garden. We stopped there for an apple cake and for the cats!
As a crazy cat lady I had to visit Cat Cabinet while in Amsterdam. Very lovely little museum, even got to pet the resident kitty. :)

Possibly one of the strangest places I've ever visited, Het Katten Kabinet is relatively small - about three rooms on the second floor of a large canal house - but (somewhat) makes up for its limited size with its utter absurdity. The 'museum' is filled, floor-to ceiling, with art about and featuring felines, with a edge of neglect that pushes the whole thing just over the edge of weird into creepy. Strangely, all of the cat art is hung over the existing furnishings of what seems to have once been a fairly well-appointed home - as if someone just couldn't be bothered to move anything before converting the space. There are real cats that live there (because of course there are - also they are friendly and will let you pet them) and a backyard you can peek at but not enter, full of cat and kitten related metal signs. The 7 euro admittance fee feels a little steep for the 10 minutes you'll spend here but it's weird enough that I wouldn't tell you to immediately cross it off your list either.  I'm not sure what I was expecting when I went - and even after going I'm not totally sure what to tell someone else, except that this is not a place to go with any expectation of learning something about cats - if anything it is a place to learn about people who love cats. A short and strange trip into the demented mind of the proverbial crazy cat lady. If your sense of humor tends toward absurdity and farce, sure, swing by real quick.. you'll probably be at least mildly amused. If you don't usually use the word 'weird' as a term of endearment ... maybe skip it?Overall - I would probably recommend this for either a) CAT. PEOPLE. or b) people feeling giggly post coffee shop visit. It would probably be best enjoyed by those who are at least a little of both.

This was an accidental find as I didn't know it existed but only saw through a 'mini- card' in my hotel (that also entitled me to a free postcard!) the price is a bit steep (7 euros) as I only spent about 15 minutes inside but it's jam packed with cats. Pictures, advertising posters, sculptures and much more. Quirky and beautiful canal side house as well. There is a small shop at the end with cat related gifts. A very pleasant way to spend a half hour or so - if you like cats that is...

We missed our cat back in Denmark, so we found this place, it made us feel a lot better. The museum isn't that big, but has everything a cat house should have, from mummified cats to alive and nice ones. Lovely for families

Quaint but wonderful little place, with a great collection of feline-themed posters, paintings, drawing, and even a few antiquities. The sole feline inhabitant was worshipping Ra from a prostrate position on a very comfy-looking couch near the entrance. Unfortunately the gift shop didn't offer replicas of the more interesting posters on display.

The Cat Cabinet is in a beautiful canal house and is the only museum in the world dedicated to art about cats. It has an excellent collection of posters, paintings, and sculptures from all over the world. My favorites were the Steinlen posters. It also has two live cats.You can see it all in about an hour. It is definitely worth the trip if you love cats!

As a cat lover, this place was amazing! So lovely and I got to sit and stroke some of the cats living there, no need to pre book either! Ideal place for cat lovers to look around at the art work; definitely recommend

We went to the museum after seeing it advertised in our hotel. We knew it was an art museum but didn't know exactly what to expect. The rooms are beautiful and are filled with drawings, paintings, pictures and sculptures of all different types of cats. When we were walking through we were also greeted by two cats who lived there and they were very friendly and happy to be fussed!

This was the weirdest place I've ever visited. Can't really make up my mind if I liked it or not - it's just a house filled with cat related artwork. The several real cats we saw along the way made it worthwhile (I was missing my cat, so this is a good way to get some purr-time if you're having withdrawal symptoms!!) The good news is that it's cheap, so good if you've got time to kill but don't bust a guy trying to visit with limited time.

In the peace of a very beautiful typical house of Amsterdam very nicely furnished and decoratedA collection on the Cat : paintings, sculptures, posters, mummy ! And real cats purring and being caressed.A good time in Amsterdam !

This is very interesting and fun to do, the staff is very nice, the house is a monument by itself and it's quite interesting to visit, i would recommend it and the price is affordable so if you're looking for something unusual to do, go for it ! (+ of course the pleasure of being surrounded by so many cute cats walking around)

It's €7 pp entry to a single floor of the townhouse containing more posters of art than actual original art containing cats. There is the small garden which too contained posters on sticks stuck into the ground - the garden was closed off due to light rain. Overall I'd seen everything in 10 minutes. Maybe less if I hadn't stroked the cat wandering around. Not what I expect for such a price.I'd have had more fun spending that €7 on a coffee and watching cat gifs on my phone.

Found this listed as things to do in Amsterdam. My wife said "Ok, we HAVE to go see this!" It was fun. All our friends were jelly when we put it up on Facebook. Ok, maybe not all of them but a good paw full. I didn't see that joke yet referenced so I took a chance. Stopped for ice-cream on the way back. The Dutch love their ice-cream and so do I.

Blitzed through this strange little museum in about 10-15 minutes. I liked it though, cat paintings, pictures, posters and sculptures crammed everywhere around this old house and in the garden which you can't go in but you can see out the window. Cost 7 Euros to go round- we only saw 1 real cat in there though which my friend was a bit disappointed about. Good if you have some time to kill and like cats.

If you love cats then you will love this place. A museum dedicated to P J Morgan a cat of great standing it seems. I have been 3 times now, this time with my husband who loved it. Every type of cat is here, sculptures, paintings, posters, furniture and even a real one lounging on the radiator cover this time. At 7€ it's a bargain & a nice alternative place to go for a hour or so. Central & near other sites too.

If you miss your cat while you're on vacation or appreciate car sensibilities this is a fun place to visit. Also if you are interested in seeing inside one of the mansions from the "golden bend" this is the only one that is open all the time. The others I believe open once a year one a special day.  It is pretty dark inside and not very big. Expect to spend maybe 45 minutes there.  The paintings and prints are displayed rather eccentrically on the walls or on standup boards in the middle of the ballroom.  There are many entertaining pieces of art. While it is hard to see some which are up quite high in dark rooms.  There are also some adorable resident cats who seemed pretty friendly.  I also enjoyed their blatant disregard for merchandise as they sat upon sample copies of books crushing the spine or slept on the tote bags for sale.  The negative review below is probably right in that it might not be a great spot for little kids? It might be boring except for chasing the resident cats which they probably won't like.  But all in all I thoroughly enjoyed it as it seemed the other people there at the same time did too. After you're done perusing the art, pet some cats!

This was the worst museum I have ever been to before.We paid 14 euros for the two of us, I really like cats and thought it would be a laugh to go.  I was wrong, after 5 minutes inside I wanted to get out.  We had to walk across a boardwalk to get into the building, as they were having work done on the floor outside.  So this made it hard to find as there wasn't any warning signs.We walked around the back garden that had a few paintings of cats and one cat fast asleep on the floor.  We went back inside to find a few rooms with  the same type of paintings and that was that.  They had a gift shop but the prices were ridiculous!Have told anyone we spoke to not to bother, the internet would give you a better tour.

I am very much a cat lover and I was so thrilled to get to go see the Kattenkabinet. It was filled with all kinds of cat memorabilia from different time periods. I bought a magnet and several postcards for all my friends. Too bad those beautiful cats inside couldn't be bought. They were so sweet and one very very large one! Highly recommended for any cat lover. The old cat cartoon was my favourite part. What was the name of that short film? I loved it!

mainly poster or paints are hang on the wall..Beware!! sometimes real cats are appeared front of you! they are so quiet and soft.... my girl, 9yr old normally scared of animals... but sh could manage to walk through front of them..maybe she didn't realised the cats were there....

I love cats and didn't mind paying the entry fee. This small MEWseum is in a central Amsterdam townhouse with old looking furniture. If you are an architecture buff, it may be worth the admission to see the layout of an Amsterdam canal town house. This museum has several dimly lit rooms full of bland CAT paintings, but the most interesting exhibits were the vintage advertising posters (several original 1900's posters by Steinlein) featuring CATs! Being a vintage poster fan, I enjoyed this collection. Reproductions of some vintage CAT posters are shown in the small  backyard garden, which makes for a good photo opp since the real vintage posters are in dark rooms inside the house. I noticed 3 (real) cats lounging around on the furniture which looked to be "period pieces". For the entry fee, I spent less than 1 hour touring the MEWseum but I believe it was money well spent.

The museum is only cat art, which is quaint, but is very small and seems to be in a re-purposed flat. There are two little cats that run around, which is sweet. As long as you know you're paying for only art about cats in a rather large area, I recommend it. However, I really would not consider it to be worth the 7 euro price they ask for upon entry.Do keep in mind there is a student concession price if you're in university. It's 4 euro. I would consider that to be a better 'normal' price for the museum entry, so if you're a university student and love cats, have at it.  Entrance is hard to find, look for a black and white sign that says "KattenKabinet" (see photos). Despite my criticisms, I do think the museum is very cute and the artwork is well cared for. Seeing a few real cats along the way was also a pleasant surprise!

I love cats and was excited about going to a cat museum. Paid 7 euros each, went upstairs, looked around at some pictures and statues...  15 min later we were looking for more that was it. Just one floor of pictures. We saw 3 cats in there, and of course a gift shop. Way too expensive for what it is. Felt really ripped off and do not recommend it to anyone.

This museum only shows art featuring cats. They have everything from statues to adverts to an original Picasso. They also have live cats sleeping in the shop and around the museum, and even a kitten when we were there. It was the friendliest, playing and sleeping on people's laps.If you go to the Poezenboot first, you get a 50% discount.

Don't like giving a bad review but there really isn't much here for the entrance fee. If you love specifically cat art it is probably very interesting but it wasn't a good plan to take 2 cat obsessed children. Like another reviewer says it is basically someones house and it seemed as if there were other exhibits (such as the garden that you could see but not get to and had a lot of cat art in it visible at a distance) that were closed off. It is very dark. Almost as dark as the mood of the young lady at the ticket office. I realised quickly that it was not what 2 kids wanted to see. One was actually scared of the ambience. With an ambling examination of the exhibits it took approx 5 mins to end up back at the gift shop/entrance which seems harsh when it has cost £20 for 2 adults and 2 kids but I freely accept it wasn't what I was expecting and I took them to the wrong place.On the plus side there was 1 cat asleep in a corner and the girls got a free visit to the toilet on the way out.This museum is probably a good measure of just how much you love cats. You have to really love cats.

This is basically someones house, but I think that actually adds to its appeal. it's a collection of cat art spread over the house, with a few cats meandering about and a nice little gift shop. It's right in the centre of Amsterdam as well so you don't have to go out of your way.

This is one of Europe's little secrets and I have to admit that I am surprised that there weren't more people here than the English family that were sitting there playing with the cats. Mind you, it is actually hidden away and if you don't know about it you are unlikely to find it, but I have to admit that this place is awesome. Basically it is a museum of everything to do with cats, and they even, not surprisingly, have some real live cats wondering around here. There are lots of posters of cat related subjects, as well as lots of figurines, and a shop downstairs where you can purchase copies of some of the posters. All I can say is that I loved this place, and you can even play with the cats (if the cats let you of course because, well, they are cats and any cat lover knows what cats are like).
The museum is for cats lovers. There are some real cats there. There are oldPaintings of cats, Old image of Disney cats, different photos. Generally interesting if you love cats.

I love cats and this place is like a glorious palace of wonder. It's full from floor to ceiling with quirky, unique posters and art work. The actual living breathing cats that you randomly find curled up snoozing on a chair in some corner of a room make it even better. The shops really good too.

So, as cat lovers, we knew going to this museum would be a priority on our trip. It was easy to find, not far from a tram stop. The museum has an entrance fee (I think 7 euros?) and is relatively small. But there is a pretty nice selection of cat themed art and happily, there are a few resident cats. The quaint gift shop has a few prints and booklets of the art featured. Definitely worth a visit for any cat lover!

A lovely old house on Herengracht stuffed full of cat related paintings, furniture and memorabilia.Don't expect to spend long here, but well worth a visit just to see inside the lovely building, and of course....if you like cats this is the place for you. Lovely posters and souvenirs in the shop too!

If you love your cats and felines, then this is a quaint little museum for you. The house has a number of levels filled with all things cat related: paintings, sculptures, movie and advertising posters, etc.There is probably a good 45 minutes here.Make sure you say hello to the resident pussy cats!

The museum has some unique posters, paintings, artwork about cats. There are live cats there but is up to the cats whether you get to meet them. I was lucky enough to meet two and one of them was a gorgeous kitten. I would definitely visit again.
Good opportunity to see inside one of the old mansion houses of Amsterdam. Quirky little museum stuffed full of cat-themed memorabilia & artwork. Reasonably priced.

This cat museum in a house off the main road is dedicated to art featuring cats. Founded in the memory of JP Morgan (a cat), it is quaint, quirky, and fun. Posters, paintings, sculptures are everywhere as you wind through the rooms. Not large and a little expensive for a small museum but definitely enjoyed.

This place is pretty cool with a lot of things about cats (objects, paintings...) even real cats! It's also cheap (4€ for students) but the visit lasts only 10-15 minutes and there are barely any writings to explain things, only visual ones.

The cat museum "kattenkabinet" was eccentric and fun. I too am a cat lover so admittedly I'm a bit biased in that regard. The museum is small - but admission fee was reasonable. It was essentially multiple rooms in the upper floor of a beautiful old canal home. There were at least 4-5 cats roaming around or napping. Fun visit with a nice gift shop on the ground floor.

I love looking at houses and also I love cats so this is a great chance to see the inside of a canal house that is dedicated to cats. Best of both worlds.This is the only canal house on the Golden Bend that is open to the public. The owner is  a cat lover so it is decorated with cat statues, paintings and a few live cats. Too bad only one floor is part of the "cabinet". You can also see the yard.
Beautiful house, beautifully decorated . A lot of different cat -themed posters and paintings , some entertaining , some horrible. You can meet real cats in a museum !Slightly too expensive.

I came here as a museum visitor, not as a residential guest. It was such a pleasure to enter this beautiful restored building and be able to see inside one of these old canal-side houses.  The range of feline art was fascinating, all the different advertising posters is so many European languages. I especially enjoyed the Russian children's video of The Cat That Walked By Himself story, and some of the beautiful sculptures.  There is a lovely view of the garden from the back windows.I had to climb the staircase to access the main displays, but the kind lady on reception carried my walking aid up and down for me.I only met one of the feline attendants who followed me round and came and watched over me while I scoured the shop, but I was told there are a total of five feline residents.I would say a real pleasure for cat and art lovers.

Really just a poorly organised selection of artefacts, paintings  and advertising posters. They have the opportunity to organise and give information but fail to do so.  I am an avid cat lover and was unimpressed by the presentation and information provided.  Would not recommend at the price, sorry.

Have heard lots of rave reviews about the cat museum but it didn't live up to expectations. There wasn't any real cats around but I did find some of the paintings interesting. Have heard there is a rescue barge of cats so will look into going there

Kattenkabinet is like it's the fancy shmancy canal house museum,  almost like a spoof of it, but with a cat motif.  It wasn't cheesy at all and really made me smile!!Recommend visiting right after you visit a canal house museum (like Van Loon).

Cool chance to see the inside of one of the most beautiful houses on the canals on part of the Golden Curve of the Herengracht----one floor of cat paintings, posters, statues and more with a few live ones running around--there is also a back yard so to speak with more art, statues and cats. 7 euros

We read so much about the Cat Cabinet, one of the most common ways to describe it being quirky. If quirky's synonymous with over rated and hyped, must be it! Was looking forward to more than vintage posters and cat pictures. Not worth the time or the price of 7 euros per head - truly disappointing.

Whether you like cats or not, it is a very nice museum. There are a lot of paintings, sculpturs about cats, also from famous painters like Van Gogh. Usually you meet some cats in the museum, who live there.

Like art? Like cats? Like your art to have cats in it? Then this is right up your alley cat. A quirky little meowseum run by some cool cats that like cats and have a cat too! From watercolours, to lamps, to pinball machines, there's plenty to get your claws into. You won't be having kittens at the prices either - €3 for students and €5 for adults. Purrrrfect if you're looking for something different to do!

Always go here when in Amsterdam love this museum as love cats but there is not to much to see it only two floors and on these floors are pictures of all different cats it  has a lovely shop were you can buy cat postcards pictures I got kat bag which I love

Our second stay in the apartment at the Kattenkabinet and we enjoyed it as much as the first time. Great central location and good value for money as each bedroom has its own bathroom and there is a separate sitting room and kitchen. The museum garden was also open and we were able to spend time with all three of the resident cats. This is the ideal place for cat lovers!

Being two cat lovers, we wanted to see this place as it looked really quirky! At 7 euros for get in, it's not a cheap museum. Upstairs there's a floor of cat pictures and posters but that's about it. Downstairs there is a shop selling posters and cards and here we saw one of the resident cats fast asleep on  a pile of bags! Nothing was going to shift that cat! No tea or coffee shop although there may be one open in the summer months?

I'm a cat lover so looked forward to this cat  museum thought it was quite expensive 7  euros to look round one floor of cat pictures and portraits, there is an outside area but that wasn't open, best thing was the two real cats
Do not go if you are not a cat fan really not worth the money. The paintings weren't interesting and not a long visit.And really expensive for what it is.

I did quite like seeing all the vintage posters of cats, but at 7 euros for about 20 minutes worth of material you need to be a real cat enthusiast, although the old canal building is nice to see too. And the attention - loving real cat residents help to fill the void of missing your feline friends back home, especially since you're not likely to see many furry creatures out and about in the streets of this crazy cyclists and tram - infested city. Great gift shop too.

We came here after Van Gogh Museum and much preferred this! It has a lovely collection of cat paintings, statues and works of art. It is set in a lovely building and has resident cats who are very friendly. Worth a visit.

Beautiful art gallery that contains various artworks of different style and substance that have cats in them. A few lovely lovely puddy cats wandering around and snoozing. You don't have to be a cat lover to go here, nice art and fair enough admission price with a poster shop downstairs again. Only downfall? Limited access to the gallery, your only allowed visit one floor really while the ropes off stairs teases you with paintings.

A small but charming museum situated in a large canal-side house. We were delighted to meet one of the resident cats asleep on a windowsill! They have a great range of postcards and postcards of cat-related art and vintage advertisements. You won't pass a whole afternoon here, but if you love cats it's a must visit while you're in Amsterdam.

The Cat Museum is small, quirky and worth the fairly low admission price. The cat related art was varied and interesting and seeing the interior of an old canal house was really cool too. This was a nice break from all the serious art museums!

The Cat Cabinet is such a charming little place! Its like wandering around the home of an eccentric millionaire cat lady. If like me you love art and cats you'll find it GLORIOUS, if not then there really won't be much of interest for you. The rooms are beautifully laid out, the walls are filled with paintings, illustrations, posters, vintage advertisements as well as sculptures and figurines dotted about. Its more of a quirky gallery than a museum! The only downside is that the museum is very small and took only around 20 minutes to see everything, there is also an outside area which I assume opens up in summer (visited in winter and it was all closed off) which looked pleasant also! There were also a few live cats sleeping around the building which was a certainly a cute feature! Lovely gift shop also! I wouldn't recommend going out of your way to find it, but if it sounds like your kind of thing and you're in the area its worth checking out!

Honestly - if you love cats, you'll love it here. If you don't, I wouldn't recommend it! We visited as museum visitors, not hotel guests. The museum is small but really interesting, and the entrance price is totally reasonable for the size. The owner apparently lives upstairs and the museum is his personal collection of cat-themed art from around the world. I found it fascinating and being cat mad I was delighted that two of the owner's cats wandered up to say hello while we were there. A totally quirky experience for anyone who likes cats.

A random collection of posters and paintings with cats in. No real logic to the display and no information about anything. The building is beautiful and worth seeing and I guess it's kind of a quirky thing to go and do! Just don't expect to learn anything!

This is a large gallery of cats and cat-related pictures and objects. There is plenty to see and a lovely garden out the back. It is easy to get to, there is also a souvenier shop. Well worth a visit.

The GF and I are cat lovers and sought out the cat cabinet. At €7 for an adult the attraction isn't expensive. It is small- though we did wonder if a floor or two were closed because of the season being quiet.Unsurprisingly, the museum is full of pictures and signs including our feline friends, and even has a real live cat who loves a little stroke!Although it is a cat museum, it is also a beautiful example of an old Amsterdam house including furniture and ceilings.Well worth a visit, regardless of cat lover or hater 🐈

Personally I loved this place. 😚 💕 My partner and I loved it and had a good giggle at the art works and collection. There is a massive ecliptic mix of items with the theme of cats. The fact that it's situated in a vintage canal house is beautiful because it's really unique. They even have resident cats! 😻 the one recommendation I have is that after I pet the cats a kid started pulling one of the cats tails to agitate it. I told the kid to stop it but maybe if there was a staff member watching the cats they can see the people who shouldn't be around animals. 💕 Thankyou for a lovely Time!

We found the Museum rather small and not so cheap.It is packed with art works  - some of them rare and interesting, but it is just one floor and it took us about 20 minutes to see everything.We thought it was a little too expensive.

You will either love this place, or hate it! We loved it - but that is because we had a cat we loved to bits, and also because we love late 19th and early 20th century poster art, especially French, Art Nouveau - which this place has in spades, as well as all the art following a cat theme. Quirky, and well worth a visit :-)

This place is really small but has quite a lot of interesting things, all cat-related. I guess it is a great place to visit for people who are particularly interested in cats or else it is very likely that you would find it boring. It has a lot of fantastic art and the house itself is really interesting, like a flash from the past. This place is definitely a nice change from a typical museum.
Nice place, not only for art but for interior, give you an idea how things were looking in the past! Also 2 live cats purring around really surprised us!

I thought was a completely charming little place. I stopped by several years ago and loved it. After the tour, I was trying to decide on several vintage cat advert posters that were on display. I ended up buying them all, at a discount "bundle price" . Priceless!! I am planning to take my husband here in May of this year, during our Tulip trip.I was not here as a hotel guest!

I liked this beautiful old canal house full of kitty treasures but I can honestly understand that this is not a attraction that would suit everyone. I loved the lucky cat pinball machine and the painting of a cat attacking its prey the most. The collection is varied and both cute and controversial sometimes even in the same artwork.

A small museum but it's cheap. There isn't much to see and most things are not too interesting. It is fun to see a museum centred around cats though.The museum shop is too expensive for my taste and for the size of the museum.

Collection is obviously born enthusiastically and heartily, but exhibition is not very viewer friendly and dusty and disordered environment tells, that enthusiasm is not sufficient to maintain the collection. We visited only the exhibition and I didn't even notice that there were any accommodation (and to be honest, it would probably be the last place to sleep in Amsterdam)

This museum is on two floors of a mansion (only the four resident cats are allowed beyond the ropes).  There are many paintings and drawings of cats, and there is a gift shop on the ground floor.  The only thing I did not like was the horrid painting of a cat with its paw caught in a trap. :(

This Cat Museum features prints, original paintings, sculptures, etc., all of cats. The owner started collecting cat art many years ago, and set up the museum to honour his own cat, J.P. Morgan. It is beautiful to roam and usually empty (except for the cat(s) asleep on the 1st floor divan!) There is every type of cat art that you could imagine, going back to prehistoric times! If you're a cat lover, it's definitely worth a visit.The down side is that it's only ONE floor of a canal house, and at 7 euros, quite expensive for the little there is to see and experience. So don't set your expectations too high, but be prepared to be surprised at how many artists have captured the lowly but lovely cat over the years.cats
With the student discount, I had to pay 4 euro. It's a small but cute museum, a lot of cat posters, adds, statues... 2 real cats as well... It's funny, but would never pay more then 4 euro.

Staying in the Kattenkabinet really added to our experience of Amsterdam. Overall it's one of the best places I've ever stayed in. The house itself is beautiful, and there's free access to the museum which is very interesting. The owners are friendly and helpful and they have 4 cats who will visit your apartment in the evenings if you wish. It's a real home from home. The apartment was spacious and warm (it has its own boiler so you can control the temperature yourself). We stayed in "Mouse" which has a double room with ensuite bathroom and a twin room with separate shower room, and a view of the garden. We would definitely stay there again.

My daughters were very excited to visit the Cat Cabinet. An interesting collection of cat themed paraphernalia from statues and magazine covers to the real thing wandering around the rooms. The gift shop had some lovely bits and pieces, and it is quite located next to some great pubs and eateries.

A largely unlabelled assortment of advertising, memorabilia and objects with a feline theme. It has curiosity value and may provide a cat-fix to those travelling and missing their purring pets. Much though I like cats, though, the exhibit felt like an opportunistic gathering together of oddities with little if any in the way of curation. It could have been a warehouse for a cat-lovers car boot sale as much as an exhibition. 2.5, and rounded up mainly because the sheer weirdness of the collection suggests someone may have made an effort to collect it all together (though quite why remains unexplained).

I went to this museum after seeing a small card of it at the lobby of my hotel.What a place!Like entering in a fairy tale world.The decoration,the light,the sculptures,the paintings,the wonderfull magic-like atmosphere!The entrance was just 7 Euros.I ll never ever forget it.It is like entering in a magic world.I recomend it to everyone also to people with kids.I regret for not buying a book they had with pictures about the museum and its story.Next time I will.Very friendly guy at the entrance.A definately great magic place to see even if you are not a cat lover.It is he atmosphere that counts.

What do you need to know? It's a museum dedicated to fine cat art. If that is not the best kind of museum, I do not know what it. In all seriousness, this truly is a wonderful way to kill and hour or so in Amsterdam. The art itself runs the gamut from simple drawings, to true paintings and sculptures. The best part however are the Cats that roam the halls waiting to be petted. Seriously, spend the 7 Euro and do this museum.

I am not a cat person so the place was not fantastic for me but OK for a quick visit and is in keeping with some of the weirdness of Amsterdam. They have lots of cat theme posters in the gift shop that make good gifts if you have a cat nut in your life and need to pick them up something. This is the place for that.The artwork is eclectic to say the least and there are some rather goofy things to see and the only requirement seems to be that cats are somehow involved in the piece. It will only take at most a half hour to go through the place, unless you are really into it. I read in one of the promo ads that there was some valuable artwork in there by Rembrandt or somebody, but if there is it is not well called out. A lot of what was in there seemed to be of "Dog's playing poker" themed quality but it was interesting overall. It was very close to my hotel so stop by if you are in the immediate area but I would not suggest making a special trip to this part of town just to see the museum.They do have a table where you can leave your own cat themed notes or artwork to add to the collection, I rather liked that.

It was raining and we were in the neighborhood, and I like cats...but, certainly not worth the 7$ entrance to see a bunch of cat posters and figurines. No real explanations or organization attempted, so I don't consider it a real "museum".

Don't expect to much, though. They have an interesting collection of all sorts of arts, posters, books, ... with cats in the title or cover picture, or ... .But that's about it. Plus 2 nice cats running around the reception.Actually, when we wnet out, we were not so sure if we had seen a cat museum or rather an architecture museum, because the house and the interior at times are even more fascinating than the exhibits about cats. ;-)

I don't know how there are any good reviews of this place. Maybe everyone else has lived a very sheltered life full of doom and gloom and other crap galleries. I am an absolute cat lover so I was quite excited about this. It was the worst 7 Euro I have ever spent. Basically someone has opened their house, filled it with a few nice paintings of cats and a whole load of terrible ones and turned it into an art gallery. There is a mummified cat that looks like it's made from papier mache. I actually might do this in my flat in Glasgow just to pay the rent. I'm pretty good at papier mache cats. Watch this space for Krappenkabinet Glasgow. It'll be cheaper though because I've not got a total brass neck.Absolutely terrible, go and buy yourself a rum and coke instead.

If you are a cat lover then this small museum is worth a visit. Museum is on the first floor with artwork and also normally a real cat fast asleep on a sofa. A little gift shop for getting all those cat posters.

Don't set your expectation too high.It's a single floor of an elaborate old Dutch house littered with very old cat related posters, art work, and objet d'art.The house is actually just as interesting as it's content. You'll not spend hours here but you should come away with a smile on your face; especially if you come across their pet cat sleeping on a chaise longue like we did.

For all cat lovers this small but interesting little museum is well worth the visit and even has a real cat that is normally asleep on a sofa in one of the rooms. Mainly full of art work featuring our feline friends. A nice little shop if you want a poster too.

We visited this on a whim, it is located on one of the most historically important canals in Amsterdam (Herengracht) it is in a beautiful old house built in 1664 and is full of works of art dedicated to cats! There are cat pictures, paintings and figures everywhere. There are even real live cats roaming around or sleeping. Some of the many posters on display are actually instantly recognisable iconic cat images and there is even a mummified cat  from ancient Egypt. This is definitely a must visit for cat lovers but, I feel the non-cat worshippers will struggle to remain interested for more than 20 minutes or so.

For all the cat lovers out there, worthy of a visit, and don't forget to pop into the shop. Great little place off the beaten track. Lovely shop to peruse with great paraphernalia to be had. Rainy day activity.

Missing my cats back home, I was gratified to be greeted by Leon and Shirley (two of the four museum resident cats) at the door.  The museum is not too big and very quirky, but is worth the money for a visit.  We oogled the cat art and spent lots of time patting Leon and Shirley.  And we bought a nice poster to bring back home.  If you are a serious cat lover, don't miss this place; you will be sorry if you did.

Lovely little museum full of gorgeous art in a traditional old Dutch house with their very own museum cat - what's not to love!? Friendly staff, quirky little museum and great atmosphere. Would recommend to any cat or art lover!

The place smells like an old house.therefore the air can get a little difficult to breath in.It's cute,but we only saw one living cat. This is a museum, not a hotel. It's quite limited for the price you pay. The art is nice.The store is great though. I would have buy it all...

I visited Katten Kabinet on a whim when someone from my hostel recommended it to me. If you have a spare 30-60 minutes and you're in the area, it's well worth your time. There's some quirky art and the history is both informative and entertaining. One major perk is the live cats that roam around throughout the museum. Don't worry, they're very friendly. :) Admission is cheap and I highly recommend anyone with even a vague interest in cats to stop by this unique place if they have a chance. It's not every day you get to see something like this! And their gift shop is lovely to boot - perfect postcards to send to friends. If I ever get to visit Amaterdam again I'd be sure to bring some people with me to enjoy it too!

I simply loved this place - it's a collection of all things related to cats, from posters, to some costumes, paintings, and I even saw an actual cat, sleeping on a desk. How delightfully appropriate!There is also a small gift-shop at the exit and I recognized in its postcards some of the most iconic cats that I forgot I even knew.

The museum itself is tiny but really nicely organised. The collection is really interesting and diverse, from vintage prints and children's drawings to actual cats! Definitely worth a visit for any cat lover, and the gift shop is adorable!

Clearly only for the cat enthusiasts!We visited here in the hope of seeing many cats wondering around and seeing a bunch of strange pieces of feline related art and sculptures. Although an enjoyable distraction from the usual Amsterdam tourist traps this place is very limited. There's a shop, a stair case and about 4 rooms containing mainly artwork but also sculptures (all cat related as you'd expect).The problem is this place was pretty pricey and we'd walked around it in around 20 minutes. I spotted 3 cats, 1 of which was in the reception with a note asking customers to let him sleep. The other 2 seemed free game to stroke!Despite trip adviser filling this under "hotel" it isn't, this is an art gallery.

This is a canal front house that has been converted into a museum focusing on artwork that primarily features our furry feline friends. Everything from a mask that was on a mummified cat in ancient Egypt to modern paintings.  The only downside to this museum is that it is quite small. The main part is located on the second floor in 3 large rooms. But what they do have is very nice. The paintings were beautiful.  This place is a must stop and see in Amsterdam if you are a cat lover.

This is a small but nice house which has been converted into a tribute to a former owner's cat, JP Morgan. The art ranges from kitschy to very nice, but if you are serious about cats-and we are-it is a delightful place to visit.Added bonus: There are multiple cats roaming the halls.

Really lovely little museum of cat art. A big selection of pieces across one floor, including cat-related artwork by the likes of Picasso, Rembrandt and Toulouse Lautrec. In addition, I met one of the cat residents, who was having a casual snooze on an armchair. Do not fear if you visit with a non-cat lover, the museum is also a beautiful example of a traditional Dutch home. And for only 6 euros (or less if you have the Iamsterdam card or have visited De Poezenboot), it's definitely worth it.

My mother and I, being the crazy cat ladies we are, decided early on that this place was a must see during our visit! It's very quaint, and a beautiful building but it's not very big, we were in and out in maybe 30mins or less. The gift shop has some lovely cat related posters and postcards.
Silly museum but fun to wander through.  Lots of old posters and other cat related items.  Worth a peek if you are a cat lover.

This museum, which featured every kind of art you can imagine pertaining to cats, was very cute and fun. The highlight of the museum was the cats who live there. I thoroughly enjoyed my visit and thought the price was very reasonable. It's not a huge museum, but it's more of a quirky hole-in-the-wall that you don't want to miss.

Suits several tastes - combines art gallery with private collection of feline curiousities but great also if you fancy a nosey inside a grand canal house. Take your time, it is calm and peaceful. There are of course real cats snoozing on sofas, getting in serious zzzzs. A great place to step out of the touristy bustle.

I didn't want to believe the bad reviews on this museum but really there nothing special abt this museum its only posters in french and details are mostly in dutch.And only 2 real cats not at all plenty running  everywhere.I was expecting much more honestly! !

You know the reviews regarding the cat Cabinet are quite accurate. It is dingy (The lighting needs work) damp and a bit grim but I think that this ads to the charm of this establishment.Sure tariff  is dubious because you only have access to one floor, and there is a serious lack of logic to the displays.There is no structure to the museum, rather, random cat pictures and figures displayed randomly with no logical cohesion.Do not get me wrong, I did enjoy the visit, although the cat museum has potential to be more than what it was.Oh and this museum's creator is an old man who loves cats. Contrary to what people think, it does not belong to a crazy cat woman.If you are looking for something different, and you love Cats, you should visit the cat museum, and decide for yourself what you think.

This is a really special place which doesn't deserve the negative reviews.The museum itself is an eclectic collection of cat artwork, it contains various pieces ranging from sculptures to posters to costumes to paintings. The space itself is very aesthetically pleasing and quaint and you feel as though the owner has put a lot of care into crafting such a beautiful area.There are a few rooms in which the artefacts are spread through, as one walks through the building you feel as though you're getting to know each cat in each painting, that you're making many new friends on your journey.What was very moving about this museum was the small shrine to J.P. Morgan (the owner's late cat whom he loved very much) it was lovely to see the gifts he was given during his lifetime and it is nice to know there is now a museum curated in memory of him.The admission is €7, €4 for students, which I think is very reasonable seeing as it is a private collection. For those that complain about the price, where else are you going to find such a collection of feline paraphernalia? I would say don't come if you are not a 'cat person'. This is obviously a collection for those who appreciate cats and understand their intrinsic artistic value as creatures. It is obviously a very personal exhibition and, as such, it should be treated with the respect it warrants.

It is a museum dedicated to cats in a beautiful old canal house. There is everything from cat statues to advertising posters with cats. While small, there is a lot crammed into the space. Easy to miss, on the 1st floor, is the shrine to the owner's first cat, bizarrely named after a famous banker. There are even a few paintings by artists such as Van Gogh, Rembrandt and Picasso.

I have a cat, and I love animals in general, so when I heard about Katten Kabinet, I had to visit!  It is, at the time of writing, €7 per adult to get in.  Although it does not take too long to see everything in there, it is a beautiful place with some weird and wonderful pieces of art.  The rooms are huge, and there are sculptures, paintings, posters and so many other cat-related items to be found.Definitely one for the cat lovers, and something different from the usual museums and art galleries in the city.

We loved the kattenkabinet. Admission felt slightly steep at the start however the wonderfully helpful staff and neat gift shop helped to justify the price in the end. Also super interesting to curious travelers, as visiting this museum also gives you a neat glimpse into a huge, beautiful house right in the middle of amsterdam. Wonderful place that I would recommend.

The Katten Kabinet museum is a typical canal house that is loaded with cat-themed artwork and decorations.  Scattered across several rooms the home also boasts some lovely architecture and furniture to complement the abundance of art.  (If you're lucky, you will also get to see or meet the actual cats in residence.)  It was a nice place to visit, I only wish it had been larger with still more cat-related materials.

I got dragged into this place by my wife because she missed our cats and it was rainy. It does not take long to walk through the rooms that are open to the public.  It is mostly just cat themed art work displayed in a restored older home.

This is a house, turned into museum (the 1st floor, the rest is a residential area of the owner). It is dedicated to a very special cat, which owner loved very much. This is a collection of cats connected objects, such as posters, statues, drawings, paintings....they even have some Picasso and Rembrandt. It is a very thoughtful collection and very nicely presented. There are some cats walking around the house and if you are lucky, you can get some cuddles. The staff is very nice and so is the owner!
We have been to the katten kabinet 3 times and it hasn't changed at all in the last 5 years. It's only small we really like it, this time we came back with our children.

I am a cat lover so thought a trip to Kattenkabinet was a must however I think the entrance fee is expensive for the small exhibition and I only stayed for about 20 minutes. The interior of the building is beautiful which is an added bonus but I would only recommend a visit to people really interested in cat art!

This is a tiny museum located in a beautifully , restored mansion.It was almost empty and we had lots of time to soak up the atmosphere of the place.  Being cat lovers we enjoyed the various artworks and the small but cute shop.

An interesting and quirky collection of cat objects/posters/paintings etc. The building itself is very beautiful and nice to see inside. The museum is dedicated to one mans cat and everything cat related! It would have been better to have had a pamphlet/guide or captions that were in English so that I could understand more about some of the objects. We were only inside for around 20mins. That said, I thought it was a bit pricey considering the size and execution of the exhibits.

I enjoyed a lot the visit.You can see different cat things;paintings,photos,sculptures,etc. Also you can see alive cats,sleeping on a couch or just passing by. The museum has a lovely view also! Loved the atmosphere there!

Perfect place for cat lovers. Me and my husband went to visit this Cat place, on arrival they have two kitties in the shop that are very friendly and also sleepy ha ha.Entrance is 7euros ,but its worth it. Every single thing in this house is dedicated to cats. From games, statues,photos and art. Loved it. It is very small which is a shame, but not busy at all.
A great little museum for cat lovers.  Full of cat art, statues, and other cat paraphernalia...there are even five real cats living here.  We loved it.
I liked the artwork and pieces in general and I am happy to pay 7€. I understand that many people complain about the size. I like it very much though!
If you like cats, pay it a visit. If you don't like cats, then why not! ;-)Not expect anything sensational, but if all else fails, just play with the kitten!

A unique in experience... in a good way. Probably the biggest collection of cat paintings and art on display in Europe - if not the world. My girlfriend (a cat owner) particularly liked stroking one of the cats who lived there. Overall it's pretty good value for money and worth visiting if you've already checked off the other big name attractions and museums that the city has on offer. The only negative is that the gift shop could do with more variety of items for sale.

This is a very small "museum" in a lovely home on one of the canals.  Mostly posters adorned the walls and a few pieces of art with cats.  There were old photos and children's cat drawings.  At least 3 live cats adorned the chairs languidly.  The posters were pretty cool, and apparently JP Morgan spent some time in Amsterdam and brought cats with him as there was a leather bound traveling kitty litter antique on display.  Enough to pay money to go?  Probably not when there is such a plethora of other places to go.  Though, I am a cat lover.

As a cat lover, I was excited about this, but it was disappointing. It's basically the second floor of a beautiful old canal house with a bunch of cat art. The art is eclectic and interesting, but there was no information in English about the history of the house, it's owner, or the art. We heard a cat meowing on the third floor, but the stairs were blocked off so we couldn't go up. If you're a cat lover, check out the Cat Boat (de Pouzenboot (sp?)) instead. It's a cat shelter on a canal houseboat. You can visit with the kitties, leave a donation, and get one of their cute t-shirts.
We really enjoyed our visit to the cat museum, if your a cat lover and/or you like art you should enjoy visiting here! Lovely old building on the side of the canal.

The cat museum was for me uninspiring, dismal and almost a feeling of a funeral parlour  for feline reverence,'  Not a happy cat vibe, but  a sombre, bizarre depiction and with  a strange video display. Although a penchant for cats, I did not freely experience any love of cats or any emotional bonding with the idea of owning one.The entrance cost is high at 6 euros, I found the staff disinterested, not connected that you may be in the place to interact with them, thus devoid of any welcome or warmth. No memento or scrapbook tickets exchanged you could get something in the cat shop.It gave me a need for something to lift my spirits, a large ' Kibbeling  and a ' Beertje' (Albert Cuijpstraat)

I was in Amsterdam for Xmas & New when my Dutch told me there now was a 'Cat Cabinet' museum situated on the Herengracht 497 wich would be just perfect for me as enormous Cat Lover! I went on the 6th of January together with my cat lover partner. On entrance we were immediately told be an unfriendly woman with a coat on behind a table in the hall of the amazing canal house that we needed to pay 6 Euro each, totally ignoring our enthousiastic ' Good afternoon'....We paid the 12 euro and i asked for a proofof payment, hoping and more or less expecting a nice cat influenced ticket as a reminder and/or to hand over to my cat lover friends in the UK. She seemed quite amazed and told us there was no such thing, instead we were told to take our coats off and hang them on a rack she was pointing at.We refused to do so and that was a good thing since it was awfully cold in the building. The 'tour' starts at a pair of stairs and the whole thing is situated on the first floor in 3 rooms of this majestic original canal house of the Herengracht. Except for the large black & white photo of Bob meijer and his beloved cat J.P. Piermont Morgan, it's all verydark, cold, macabre and far from impressive. ..but we did like the beautiful ceilings and large windows looking our over the canal. Once back downstairs there's a small room functioning as'shop' with strange items, such as 3d place psychadelic place mats????Not even with a cat on it..And if you need to visit the toilet, please take a torch, your own toiletpaper and somebody to stand in front of the cupboard like door...12 euro's buys you 2 large bags of Patat with Mayo!

I loved this museum. I think my husband who is not an absolute cat freak, also loved this museum which boasts of even having a Rembrandt and a mummified cat. I think the art work changes from time to time as I did not see some of the art in the pictures posted here.It is relativity small, but there is a lot to look at in this small space. For 6 Euros, I did not think the price was ridiculous and would recommend this museum to anyone just looking for something fun and quirky to do, especially if you are a cat lover.There was also the resident cat that i got to pet, which was nice.

Really not worth the money at all!  Pictures of cats just thrown together within a house.  Feels like the home owner keeping up appearances but has to resort to accepting paying guests.  Just like many upper class places in the UK!
Not very big, but they have thousands of cat artworks there are a number of cats you can pet and if they are feeling in the mood let you snuggle with them.  A must see if your a cat lover.
I loved the quirkiness of this museum. Don't go there if you don't like cats or are allergic to cats because its all about cats and there is even a resident feline at the museum.

Not only is the neighborhood an adorable place, but the Kattenkabinet is a great stop for cat lovers visiting Amsterdam. CATS GALORE! Even ones you can pick up, pet, and snuggle. Reviews claim that there are 4-5 cats in the building, but my sister and I only saw one. But one was enough, because he let us snuggle him and proceeded to follow us through the whole museum, jumping on displays and mewing when we didn't pay him enough attention. The museum itself takes about 30-45 minutes to go through, longer if you watch the quirky Russian cartoon (just hit play on the DVD player). Enjoy interesting and quirky artifiacts from all over the world and some very interesting paintings, including a 18th century battle scene where the cat appears in some demonic clouds. The art doesn't have any tags or markers on it, so you don't really know where it comes from, but it's interesting nonetheless. You can't beat a museum that lets cats crawl everywhere. If you have allergies, take your meds before hand. The museum isn't dirty, but if you're sensitive to cat dander you may sneeze a little.

The cat museum was interesting....if you are a crazy cat lady.  We were there for 15 minutes and not really sure it was worth the price of the tickets.  However, we did find a friendly kitty sitting in the window of the gift shop who was very amenable to pets and scratching - so that was somewhat worth the trip!

One of the smaller museums, not too pricey and if you have a Mini-Maps card you'll get a free postcard (Cat Themed).There's 2 floors of cat art-work, and that's it. From all different eras, artists and medium. It's not a must-see, but if you like cats it's nice. They have a real one downstairs too.

If, like my boyfriend, you are a crazy cat person or you're into art, like myself, then this museum is an absolute must. It's a great little collection and a fantastic opportunity to see inside a lovely canal house. We felt the entrance fee was very reasonable and spent a fair bit of time in there. I don't think we saw another tourist whilst we were in there so you really get a chance to enjoy the art and also have a giggle because let's face it, cats are funny creatures! xx

Quaint and quirky little museum with a lot of pictures of cats! Some notable pieces and a magnificent house - it won't take you long to look around unless you want to linger - but it is worth a visit, and there are lots of lovely posters to purchase after… The kids loved the welcome from the cat curled on the sofa in the entrance hall.

I went here with my three year old-daughter. She loves cats and therefor this was a good spot for her. It shows a nice collection of cat stuff: paintings, sculptures and lots of prularia. The building itself is also really nice to see: original old Amsterdam gentleman's house. It is something to visit in 30 minutes when you pass by.

For those cat lovers this is a museum you must visit. Interesting building, art work and exhibits. It is very close to the Bags and Purses Museum so you can do both very easily within the day. Both are charming museums concentrating on a single subject matter.

I was pleasantly surprised at how good this museum was.  It is a must for cat lovers as if you don't like cats or are indifferent, it probably isn't for you.  The best part was the real cat who just wanted to be loved.

I randomly stumbled across this place while browsing the internet for activities in Amsterdam. We only spent a day in the city, but this went at the top of my "must-do" list. If you are a cat person, it will not disappoint! If you're not a cat person, well then I just feel bad for you in general. Haha. But seriously, this is a cool little spot with quirky cat art. There is cat art by many well-known artists in addition to more obscure pieces. We also met a resident kitty hanging in the lobby. He was quite a welcome sight to someone missing her own kitties.

My wife is a cat lover I am ok with them we have two at home. This was a bit too pricey for what it was nothing special to see unless you are really into strange cat art. BTW don't know why here on TripAdvisor it is listed as a hotel it is a museum.

The museum is very interesting. It is a new sort of format for a museum, because not many museums focus on one particular theme and have collections ranging from random artists, sketches and sculptures, as well as works by art giants such as Rembrandt and Picasso. The atmosphere is wonderful, and the house itself is fascinating. The guy who owned J.P.Morgan cat is present at the museum, he was there when we came - interesting to ask him questions. Good shop downstairs, but not that many of the works are on the postcards, but there are also posters. The museum is also available as an apartment - very cool! We stayed as a family of four and really enjoyed the stay. It also gives you the option of going to the museum whenever you during the open times, without having to pay for the visit.

We started the day at van gough Museum with a wild 2 year old which didn't mix well so to find this gem was great. We had no one following us which I liked the freedom to explore and 2 year old loved all cat pictures, she could run around be herself . Even Leon the cat wasn't bothered by her my 2 cats run a mile when she is around. He let her stroke him and didn't budge I loved all mix of new and old. All different pictures, the cat mummy, the old paintings etc etc How grand theses old canal houses are and you really don't get to see theses really. So house is added bonus to cat stuff. I read reviews which says €6 is too much but I don't think it is at all. You pay $15 up for other museums. Owners have to keep house going as a house like this does keep a hell of a lot  of money to keep going.Also comment of cat been thin not been looked after right! the cat is old and is well looked after, you can see a mile off all 3 cats are treasured. When cats get old they do look a bit shabby.Sum is up a great building, nice staff, love the real cats and all cat stuff! Well recommended visit if you like cats

I love cats and anything cat related, so of course I fell in love with this attraction. It was great seeing all the cat paintings and cat figurines. There was even a live cat that enjoying being petted a lot. A must-see for cat lovers everywhere!

We went here because my kids love cats. The admission costs were high and although some of the exhibits were clearly very special like the cat mummy, we still felt that the price was to high for what you got. The setting of the museum, in a beautifully restored canal house, is lovely though.

If you love cats and want something a little different, give the Cat Cabinet a try.  It's a small building located on a canal and is full of cat posters and art.  You can purchase postcards and posters.  It was  a fun diversion during our trip--a great stop for cat lovers.

My travelling companion was very keen on visiting the Katten Kabinet!We were not disappointed by the collection of eclectic pictures and vintage advertisements that were all of course cat related! The Museum is three large rooms set in a beautiful old house in old Amsterdam. The ceilings had frescos painted on them and there were views of a large beautiful garden. For the modest entry fee it was worth going to see some unusual art work and even two real cats! The gift shop sold a very large selection of prints; mostly of vintage advertisements with you guessed it ... cats!
We were just walking by and went into this amazing private collection of cat related art from all periods of history.  Wonderful!

Don't expect anything exceptional here. The collection is very nice and consists of nice posters and small statues. But even if it was only for this lovely building interior, it is definately worth a visit.

...but maybe better for cat-crazy people (I was with two). The suggestion in the guides is that this is about art but it's mainly a collection of knick-knacks about our furry feline friends. Quite small too. Look out for the space in one room where people leave their cat-related messages in most languages for, well, I'm not sure why. But some are amusing not to say bizarre. Mieow!

This was a fun, unexpected stop. It doesn't have that clean museum feel to it. There were many large pieces on the floor, covering each other. The walls were very full in some places and it was very dimly lit. But it was none the less exciting when the live cats were walking around the cat art. Very interesting to see Picaso and Rembrant's work in this little place. I wouldn't make it a priority unless you want to say you've been to a cat museum!
It's 6 euro for less than 3 dusty rooms upstairs, filled with weird posters and odd art works, not very related to the subject. Expected much more. Not worth your money.
A museum devoted entirely to cats!  So if you are a cat lover, this is a great way to spend an hour or two.  And it's on the Herrengracht, which makes for a lovely walk.

We were disappointed in this. We expected some heart warming original paintings  and sculptures of cats but it was mostly posters  involving cats and different odd things , spread over two floors,  but we didn't feel the many of works were very original and it didn't really engaged us cat lovers enough.
Loved the quirky cat art and memorabilia. Nice to visit a different type of museum rather than just art or history

It’s a charming museum that you can see in about an hour.  Besides many the cat posters, paintings, and sculptures, there was a special exhibit (Foujita), a table of letters to cats, photos of celebrities with their cats, and the museum cat herself. Visit included in the Iamsterdam City Card.

Basically a collection of cat art and posters. Only go if you love cats though. I enjoyed it, especially the section on the founders own cat. Had some fun posters and some art by artists such as Picasso. Won't take you long to go round and we made the inevitable purchase of a cat poster. The hotel cat even joined me for my trip to the ladies - that's service!

We chose this museum as we are cat lovers and wanted a museum off the beaten track. We found it here. The museum is housed in lovely old canal house that is in fact someones home. The 2 for one deal of viewing the canal house and the cat art was a win -win. The cat art is interesting including some well known artists. its small enough not to be over whelming. If you love cats... you must visit this along with the cat boat on the singel canal.

We decided to visit the Cat Museum because it's in one of the canalside merchants' houses, and the other tours of those were far more expensive. And we weren't disappointed - we were able to get a really good idea of the opulence and scale of the inside of these vast homes. The cat museum itself was certainly a bit on the odd side, it basically feels like an extensive personal collection of cat-related memorabilia, some of it very valuable indeed, much very strange, and some genuinely beautiful. It's quite interesting but tucked away in one corner was a sort of shrine to a cat who had once lived on the property, including a bottle of wine he'd been bought and a collection of dollar bills which had been printed with his picture on them. A cautionary exhibit, perhaps, of what can happen if one becomes a little too dependent on one's cats for company.

The Kattenkabinet was a great stop on a rainy day in Amsterdam! The cat memorabelia and the real cats made my day! Also, the museum is inside a real canal house so you can see what one looks like on the inside. It was a really good, relaxing stop. Also, it's just a short walk to the tram as well.

We went here on the spur of the moment and we really happy we did!A fabulous building in its own right-one of those places you think you can do in 5 minutes but the more you look,the more you see.Loved the posters and prints but also the fab paintings.Definitely for the cat mad!

this was a lucky find - it's a compact little museum, out of the way and blissfully quiet compared to the 'bigger' attractions. The collection is varied and fascinating, from Art Nouveau prints to sculptures, postcards and sketches, pottery and jewellery. Perhaps my favourite feature was the desk covered in notes from visitors around the world, sharing the names of their own cats.It's also really nice to get a feel of what the lovely canalside houses look like from the inside, including a view out onto the gardens. I wasn't lucky enough to meet the resident cats, but I hear they make great tour guides.

After dealing with the crowds at all the mainstream Amsterdam attractions, we were glad to visit the Kattenkabinet.  In addition to all the cat-themed artwork, the house itself is very ornate and historic.  Check out the restored fresco on the second floor ceiling.  And if you're really adventurous, check out the second floor restroom - it's tiny!  The quirkiest item we saw there was the Louis Vutton portable litter box that once belonged to J.P. Morgan.  There were also some interesting posters for sale in the gift shop - nice unique gifts for cat-loving friends back home!

This museum is little known but a real gem to visit, especially for cat lovers! It's situated in a beautiful 17th century canal house-all the rooms of the museum have original beautiful decor, with all forms of cat art represented; painting, sculpture, potter,prints, etc. Bonus is a view at the back of one of Amsterdam's beautiful gardens. I find the entry fee reasonable and well worth a visit!

The collection of cat related art is interesting, weird, and funny. The building is worth a visit if you want to see how the rich people lived in this area. You can take a look at those hidden gardens (but not allowed to go out). I don't find the entrance fee (6 EUR) too much, but it's true that for 8 EUR you can enter the more sophisticated Willet-Holthuysen Museum.If you are a cat lover, this place is definitely recommended. If you don't like cats but need to follow someone who does, you will probably still enjoy this nice building.

Preferring quiet to crowds, and more obscure attractions for a different experience, I found myself here. I had a little trouble finding it, but once I was there everything went smoothly enough. Entry fee is a little steep, some might say, but just marginally if so, or one can look at it another way, all this cat related memorabilia has been collected and nicely placed around this building. For me actually, perhaps the building itself is the most charming thing. I quite enjoyed the internal architecture and walking through the rooms. Pictures of cats and other cat related things surrounding the place just enhanced that somewhat. There were also a few cats just walking around or sitting on the furniture, for additional cat effect. It's not a huge place you can spend hours in but you can take your time and though I didn't try it, I suppose it might be a cosy couch in an enticing room, to sit and read a bit of your book in. There is a little gift shop also, but what you get doesn't seem to quite justify the prices in most cases. I did want to check out the Van Gogh museum but the queue outside it was terrifying. The cat museum was a good substitute.

This place is also known as the Cat Museum. It is sited in a canal side house along the Herengracht, which means it was (or may still be) a house of the gentry (a wealthy merchant). If you look beyond the cat paraphernalia and see the actual decoration of the house you will see this is a house of quality. Fine plaster work and artistically painted roundel ceiling scenes. You are only allowed to walk around the first floor, but this place had a ballroom !Back to the cat stuff. The museum is not a destination in itself as you will only be there for about 30-40 minutes, mainly taking photographs and finding the best angle. I recommend that you combine your visit with the "Museum of Bags & Purses" and the "Holthuysen Museum", both of which are along the Herengracht. If you are a "speedy" tourist you may also be able to visit the Hermitage on the same day. Use tram 9 from the Centraal Station.The Kat Kabinet is really unusual, no matter where you look you will see something that is cat related. Painting, posters, ceramics, sculptures; you name it, if it is cat in it, it will be here.As other reviewers have stated, it is a great place for cat lovers. But if after having one of those canal cruises you want to look inside one of the those houses, this single floor will not disappoint.

We liked this place.  Okay, so we are cat lovers so you would expect that!  Basically, it is an old canal side house (beautiful on it's own) and it is filled with cat artwork.  There are also (at least) 3 kitties that call the place their home.
Loved this little museum. The highlight was definately the 3 resident cats who seemed in their element welcoming visitors.

Interesting and vast collection of curiosities. Make sure to keep an eye out for the resident kitty! Not the biggest place in the world - don't enter under the impression that you'll be in the place for hours and entertained by someone. It's a place to wander and peruse in your own sweet time. Make sure to take a scrap of paper - lovely little sideboard in one of the rooms that's full of little drawings and notes that visitors have left behind. Spent a while reading them and leaving our own. I do have to wonder if the resident kitty is trained to lead people to the gift shop though?? He did with us and we ended up spending 20 Euros on artwork for our house!

Lots of odd cat related pictures in a really cool old canal house. There's also some museum cats lounging around who are quite happy to be petted!Perhaps a little overpriced for the size, but definitely fun!

Having two cats at home, we were very interested in this and had a great time looking at the weird artwork and the various cat bits and bobs. The best bit was seeing the real life cats wandering about and knocking lumps out of each other! They were very playful and enjoyed being clapped while they ripped apart the catpets and furniture.
Lovely little place full of stunning pieces. And the best part? The 2 resident cats! Go and visit the poezenboot first for a discount on entry :)

We came across the card for the Kattenkabinet in the foyer of our hotel over breakfast.We were on the last leg of our holiday and missing our furry friends back home and I thought this would be a bit of fun, and it was!We were greeted at the front desk by the resident kitty ( lots of awws! ) and made our way around the through the old canal building.  Posters, original art, statues, photography, even a drawing by Picasso.  Plenty to see .. even a poster from the first movie I ever saw: The Cat from Out of Space!On our way out I purchased some posters from the gift shop and my boys were allowed to select a complimentary postcard each to keep.  More on Amsterdam here: http://hbfotografic.blogspot.com.au/2013/06/amsterdam.html

This is most definetly one of those small, "weird" places you have to visit whilst getting lost in Amsterdam. Had such a fun time there, paintings, statues, decorations, small items, everything in there was about cats! And we saw maybe 6 or 7 cats sleeping and walking around in there. If you find that cats are close to your heart, you will lose your heart to this place! :)

If you are a cat lover, PLEASE go. There's an extensive collection of everything cat - posters, pictures, drawings, articles, statues, artwork etc! All of it housed in a gorgeous house with wonderful interior and architecture - it's like you were back in the 1700 or 1800s! It was a bit hard to find but a friendly local helped us out, so don't be afraid to ask for directions. Just remember that the odd numbers are on one side and the even numbers on the other side of the canal. The store is wonderful as well if you want posters to bring home, postcards and so on. There were even 3 lovely kitties you can play with, which were definitely the highlight of the place!

Fun, quirky and unique. It's full of all things cat. There are statues, paintings and posters. Lots to look at and it gives you the chance to see inside one of the beautiful old canal houses. The rooms still have the amazing staircase and furnishings that feel a little like you've stepped back in time. For €6 I thought it was pretty good :) Apparently there are lots of cats wondering about, I only saw one having a good catnap while I was there. There's a gift shop where you can buy cat-featuring posters and postcards to your hearts content. If you like cats... it's definitely worth going.

Cost €6 which we thought was very reasonable. Lots of intresting cat related things to look at, and also the best bit is cats roam the museum and theyre all very friendly and like to be petted. Inside a large house so it was good to be able to see the inside of a hisorical building too. Has a gift shop with loads of posters and postcards of cats. Worth a visit but not the sort of place you would visit twice.

Lots of old paintings and pictures of cats. I am a cat person, but this was boring. There was 2 cats there, but did not get to cuddle with them as someone else already got to them first ;( nothing interesting in the "gift shop" either. Not worth going here. Only thing interesting is seeing an old Amsterdam house from inside.

My partner and I have two cats and love everything cat-related. We were very excited to visit the Kattenkabinet and knew what to expect. Yes it is a bit small but the 6 euro fee is not extortion either as long as it keeps this place open. There were some very beautiful paintings and ornaments and the old house was just impressive - especially the *dining room.*The gentleman selling the tickets and the souvenirs was incredibly unfriendly though, I don't know how you can be if you work in this place. Unfortunately the live kitties were not out for us, we were probably too early! we bought some postcards - one free one with a coupon we got from our hotel - and a great poster of an old advert featuring cats for a reasonnable price.

Just got back from a visit to this delightful small museum. Pleasant canal side setting. Charming old house. Soft and friendly cats to address. Varied and interesting posters and paintings. Highlights are the posters by Steinlen, well explained and visually pleasing, for example an advertisement for the Paris vet clinic Chekon, with the artist's daughter surrounded by cats and dogs.
