A quiet corner commemorates the cat companion of the owner, who was once commemorated on American bank notes, a special present to him! (Limited edition, of course).Good cards and posters in small shop. Pleasant and helpful staff. A lovely place to visit it you love cats. Shall think of it often.
Cat lovers worldwide, Rejoice! This museum is possible a blessing to our kind, with many cats to pet and cat-ifacts to see, i was in my element/

Small but fun museum for the cat lover. Everything you could imagine relating to our feline friends, including posters, pictures, sculptures, even a small section dedicated to one of the owner's previous cats. There is a shop selling cat-themed items on the ground floor, and a few resident real-life moggies!

Hidden away in this small 4 room museum are works by Picaso and Rembrant which are displayed modestly along with many more cat-related paintings and art-objects, if it wasn't for a small perspex plate beside them, I could have easily missed their origin. Obviously, there are quite a few house cats about as well :)While the museum was great fun, 6 EUR for a 15-20 min visit is a bit of a steep price to my taste. If you can enter for free using a city/museum card (and love cats) this is a must. Otherwise, take the price under consideration.

Day three in Amsterdam began with French toast for breakfast, followed by a short stroll to the Katten Kabinet (cat museum). The Cat Cabinet offers a wide look at the role of the cat in art and in culture through the centuries, and was a real treat for me. Everything here is cat related; there were even two resident cats in the foyer. The Cat Cabinet was founded in commemoration of the ginger cat John Pierpont Morgan (1966-1983). This special cat was the stubborn, headstrong companion of the museum's founder, Bob Meijer.The only downside was that the gallery was really rather small, and only kept our attention for half an hour or so.

Capitals needed for a cat lover like me! I loved it! A lovely little house, full of old/new ornaments, real cats, posters, videos.. you name it, they've got it! Not expensive either, and they have a lovely little shop full of cute catness!

We were met in the hallway by a lovely (real live) tabby cat that the receptionist told us was the owner’s 18 year old treasured pet. Making our way up the stairway we began the tour of this relatively small but interesting museum. Being feline lovers it was great to see the appearance and portrayal of cats in various artistic formats, posters, paintings, sculpture and porcelain.It was also a good opportunity to see the interior of a grand canal side house.

The friendly woman gave us free entrance because we arrived half an hour before closing time. The house is beautiful and relaxing place to be. the paintings are stunning. 2  cats were sleeping  peacfully on the couch! i would recommend it to my friends

Nobody who came to this museus mentioned the amusing Russian animation of Kipling's "The CAT that walked by Himself" from his 'JUST SO STORIES'. I had to come thrice to the museum to see it.. By itself, it is a brief and comprehensive (pre)history of how savage man defended himself from the bison, the mammoth, the saber tiger, and later tamed the wild animals (wolf into dog, horse, cow) for his service and their nurturing and protection. How woman tames wild man, and finally.... enter the Cat that walks by himself, who cunningly tames the witchy woman to his convenience, not without offering a triad of services. Yet, fails to befriend man and dog. But still, He is the Cat that walks by himself and all places are alike to him. Superb.  And yes, cute little living cats sleeping in Louis XV divans and couchces do not seem to respond to human stroking, as though they were drugged by the hemp tourists themselves consume in Amsterdam. Hey, but don´t forget, cats walk on their own and all places (and people) are alike to them.  This is Lorenzo da Firenze, from Florence and Mexico City.

I am a huge cat lover, so I was a bit excited to go in here. I was sadly very disappointed.After paying 6€ for entry, we walked up the stairs to discover that the museum consisted of three rooms and a small hallway. Despite the fact that the old house itself was very nice, we left feeling like it was a waste of money. The lady at the desk was also less than enthusiastic about being there, which did nothing to help the situation.

Great little museum, four beautiful rooms and hallways displaying cats in art and commercials. Interesting  to see that Picasso sucked at drawing cats... we were also welcomed by three residentials that lived in the house. Three very friendly and cuddly striped cats thar made their very best to make us feel welcome. They had their own very special waterhole in the museum. Go see..... We loved it!

As cat lovers, when we saw this mentioned in our guide book we thought we had to visit. The disappointment started when we arrived. It consists of a small collection of posters and other cat artefacts in 3 rooms of a house. It was so dull that I am really struggling to remember any artefacts to mention in this review! A cat-themed pin-ball machine was the highlight of the visit. Other than that it is just a collection of pictures and ornaments, most of which are very shabby. There were 2 live cats in reception as we left, but I was concerned about how thin and under-nourished they seemed. When I stroked them they were like bags of bones. They were more like strays than cared-for pets.I suppose you could possibly consider this to be a quirky or eccentric place for cat lovers and accept it as that. However when you compare the admission cost of EUR6 against EUR10 to get into Anne Frank’s House I think I am justified in expecting quite a lot more. This place is not worth visiting and the people who run it should not be encouraged.

This museum is located in a house at the side of a smaller canal and is not very big. However it is stuffed full with different cat-themed artwork, some of it better than others but all decidedly feline. There were some artworks by renowned artists on display, including a Rembrandt drawing and a Pablo Picasso drawing, and the works by less famed artists are also fun.My mum and I visited this museum whilst it was being re-organised, and the staff members moving displays around were quite loud and rude, ignoring the fact that they still had visitors in the museum. At one point one of them knocked a painting off the wall and then laughed.There are some displays which feel a bit like they're mocking the visitor - like a cat made out of a cardboard box and tape, and a statue with a pompom jacket on - but these might also just be a sign of curators who really like anything to do with cats. This museum is fun and indulges cat-lovers, so I hope it is the latter.

I absolutely loved the posters and artwork in this museum, but I can see where others might not be so inclined.  Please only go to this museum if you are extremely interested in cats and cat art.  This is also just a small museum, so don't expect hundreds of items.  It's just an old house, 2 or 3 stories of small rooms with lots of cat posters and some cat sculptures and paintings.

I'm glad I went but I'm also glad I had a discount for the entry.  There are just a few rooms but they are crammed full of every possible type of cat poster, ornament, etc.  The rooms themselves were interesting for the old architecture.  Two of the resident kittens were present which also made it worthwhile.

My husband and I are both Cat lover's so decided to visit this museum. I would have given the 3 but a price of 6 euro's seems so steep for this museum.It is a small museum with interesting artifacts, ornaments and pictures and poster's of Cats. It is spread over three rooms. There we no descriptions or facts or anything written about cats anywhere in the museum, which was a disappointment.My main bug bearer was the price, 6 euro's is very steep when you consider you can visit the houseboat museum for half the price, and it is a very similar experience.

This museum offers a rather small collection considering the entrance fee. Also, the live cats kept here are apathetic, which I found downright insulting :) They have some interesting exhibits, and of course the interior of the building is beautiful and the museum provides great photo-ops.

On entering this fine example of a canal house,I was in awe of the artwork,the high ceilings and being sent back to time of days gone by.This place is an incredible time capsule ,and worth it for the history.The art work is all about cats.Great examples and posters available afterwards.The resident cats plated to their audience.This place was used in Oceans 12,as Catherine Zeta Jones house....purrfect for such a sex kitten!

Though it will take less than an hour, impression is great. One can admire not only with numerous paintings and figurines of cats, but with the atmosphere of the house in the richest part of the Amsterdam
I like,love and adore cats! But this was the most awful place I have ever visited. The building- so dark and unpleasant, noting special ABSOLUTLY NOTHING! Money down the drain.

Being a sucker for the weird and wonderful means this place is well worth the visit. I a city full of great and renouned museums and attractions this place is a little gem. Note any distaste or dislike for feline kind should avoid. Obvious, but must be said

Do you remember the artwork of Al Stewart's 33rpm "Year Of The Cat"? Kattenkabinet will bring you to the same atmosphere. A wonderful little cat-themed museum that will bring a big smile on feline lovers’ faces. Beautifully decorated, arranged and last but not least, romantically located. If you are lucky, you can have some playful time with one of the three real cats (two young twins and a kind elegant 17 years old lady cat) who will greet you and sleep on your knees purring when you sit. They're the real stars of the place. :)The lady at the counter is helpful, smiling and obviously loves cats. For everyone who loves cats and art.

Slightly ramshackle cat museum in a beautiful canal mansion.  The exhibit itself was very good (impressive to see a Rembrandt and a Picasso in a random individual home).  But, more intriguing is the house itself -- with large parts of it in the same condition as 100 years ago or more.  Several of the rooms have period detail from even earlier.  My wife is a cat fanatic so getting to play with some of the house cats was nice for her.

I quite enjoyed this little museum.  The building itself was quite nice and still felt pretty homey in a cluttered sort of way.  If you haven't been in a canal house this is a good example and you can enjoy the canal views pretty much on your own as the museum isn't very busy.  The collection of cat art is eclectic and amusing.  The main business is a hotel and the museum is run more as a sideline.  Not very expensive though and recommended if you are a cat person.

First off, let me say that I have been to 3 cat museums in my life and that I am also pretty much an expert on presenting cats in the most artistic way because i paint and draw cats myself. This kabinet was definitely top notch. The artistry was im-paws-ibly diverse. My only complaint was that the live exhibit was unresponsive to my stroking. Also, I would have enjoyed many more live exhibits to stroke. Some of the art was worse than mine but hey, the more cats the better. In conclusion, it would be a cat-asstrophe if you skipped the Kattenkabinet!

We went here as we saw it on trip advisor and thought it was something different, being cat owners ourselves. ItTook a bit of finding and did not open till midday so we had a coffee and went along at 12. Rang the bell And were admitted, it was slightly haphazard and we were told upstairs was the museum and downstairs was the shop. Off we went. Now as a cat type museum it was slightly interesting, there were many nooks and crannies to investigate with some choice pieces and information there if you could find it. Mostly cat advertising posters and some art and sculptures. There's a small corridor where you can find out about the ginger Tom who inspired it all and his life. Thought that could have been a bit more prominent though?The main thing I want to point out is the building itself, what a lovely place, painted frescos on the ceilings, great rooms and furnishings, we thought it was much better than the more expensive museum van loon. The shop was disappointing thought and it kind of seemed like it was open as a hobby rather than a business. Great building though. Worth 6 euros for that..

I love Cas and was looking forward too visit that place! I expected more than 3 rooms with an overcrowding of paintings, statues and different little things about Cats. That was sweet but not that interesting and we have only seen one little (and cute) cat... who has been scared by the screamings of the childs that live there with several other people! Dont you know that this was also the actual house of people? Now you do: everything is closed by "no access/Private" signs... They said "5 rooms with art and cats blablabla" and i was ok to give 6€/person, but for in fact "3 very small rooms, a hallway and a souvenir shop" i will never have paid so much! First time i am disappointed by something about Cats :( If you really love Cats, go to the Poezenboot: Its free, lots of cats and its for a good cause!!!
I mean, if you really like cats, you should try it! It is not very big, but there are plenty of posters and paintings and sculptures featuring cats. And there are real cats, too!

Initially the reason for visiting the cat cabinet was more for a laugh (to be able to post on facebook that we took our partners to a cat house).What a pleasant surprise it turned out to be. the museum housed a collection of works by numerous painters including Toulouse L'Autrec and Picasso, also a collection of posters which according to the guide dated from 1860.Unfortunately we didn't see any live felines but were rewarded with a painted ceiling from 1670.Admission is a bit high 6 euro for adults but you get a 50% off voucher with a brief history of the building ( which can be used at a later date for the holder of the voucher).

This odd little museum is housed in a beautiful old mansion.  The ceiling work in the 4 or 5 rooms is stunning.  there are all kinds of cat art and objets de art in this museum, along with a real cat or two.  Thirteen year old daughter enjoyed looking and holding a cat.  The museum was okay for a 30-45 minute break.

There is really nothing to see there. It is just a big old style appartement with cat pictures and painting on the walls. The house is amaising but the museum itself is absolutely out of date and there is nothing to see there..
I loved the paper machÃ¨ and the toilet paper in the scratching post is a must see!The santuary of morgan, the owner's best friend (a cat) who has past away is lovely.We too trust no dogs!

Paid 4.5 Eu. (discounted price with Amsterdam I-Card) should have been free, and there is not much to see.  Only 1 floor with a few rooms of mostly artwork and posters of cats.  I was expecting more and at least a few live cats to add to the experience.  I asked where they were hiding, and was told that the 2 resident cats were away for the weekend.  Suggestion: get more live cats and add another floor.  It is close to a main canal, and the Handbag museum, which I actually liked, even though I'm a guy and a cat lover, and was free with Amsterdam I Card as well as many other well know museums such as the Van Gogh.

Really looking forward for a visit, I was very disappointed. And I'm a cat lover. You find some posters on the wall, some cat statues and that's it. Costed me 6 euros, which was about 1 euro a minute. There was some exposition on Fiep Westendorp's paintings, which are hardly known outside of the Netherlands itself. I rather hoped to find some explanations about catlife and so on, but I didn't.

I looked quite a while to find this place, then paid the €6 entry not knowing what to expect. A few rooms with pictures of cats on the walls and a few ordinary cat statues was what my €6 had bought. I was very disappointed and left thinking that I could put together a better cat museum myself.

Rather small and relatively poorly located, it is interesting, especially for cats lovers.We noticed a graphic by Rembrandt, but we wanted to see real felines, in flesh and bones. It was only one, a beautiful very old one (17 years)!It is good to visit on a rainy day...

If you love cats you have to visit this museum, it is tiny but very well decorated, it has some fine art about cats, some very old posters, statues and what not!! If you are lucky you will also get to see the sweet little 17 years old cat that belongs to the owner of the museum!! We own 3 cats so we were curious about this museum and it didn't disappoint us!!

I completely agree with Glyntrepid - this museum is overpriced for what it offers and is very unwelcoming (not sign-posted, have to ring doorbell to gain access, disinterested staff). There was someone in the music room banging out some Chopin, seemingly affronted that anyone had the temerity to visit!The collection of paintings is quite pleasant, but the lighting is so poor that it is hard to appreciate them.  The vivid colours of the Tarkhoffs were particularly wasted in the music room - much better to view them on the KattenKabinet webpage. The Steinlen posters fare better, but haven't we seen these all before on greeting cards?  As for the Rembrandt etching - you need a magnifying glass to spot any feline presence! Probably the best exhibits are the lovely sculptures, particularly those by Sandoz - these raised my rating from one star to two.It is always nice to mooch around in the splendid canal houses of the Herengracht, but there are plenty of better properties than this to to visit.  If you are looking for a great museum in a golden age canal house it is much better to opt for the Handbag museum.Maybe if there had been some real cats in attendance I would have felt more positive - obviously at least one cat does live there because we saw a litter tray. I quite liked the quirky memorabilia of the ginger moggy J.P. Morgan (particularly his Louis Vuitton travelling litter tray!)Anyway, our visit was pleasant enough, but did not justify six euros.  It was particularly galling that I couldn't use my museum card and my daughter could not get any reduction with her student CJP pass.  Also be warned - don't expect any toilet facitities or cafe.

I'd really looked forward to this but was so disappointed. It was SIX Euros - which was at least 5 times too much. The woman who seemed to be running it was - to put it politely - aloof. After ringing the doorbell and being let in - you're left to your own devices. basically you go upstairs where there are a couple of rooms full of some posters with cats on and a few feline ornaments. The house itself is also quite interesting as it gives a glimpse inside one of the golden bend properties. To be fair, if I'd paid a Euro or two, I'd have thought it was a harmless bit of fun. The fact that it was all rather cold and unwelcoming only added insult to injury. As very much a "cat person" I felt this place really let the side down. Your average dog enthusiast would pounce on the chance to say it was typically cat-like - cold, snooty and distant. A real let down - don't bother!
The house is spectacular and is filled with some wonderful cat inspired art. You may even get to meet one of the feline residents too...Fantastic!

I visited a couple of years ago after reading about it in a guide book and  I'm so glad we did.  On a trip with two young teenage girls it was a breath of fresh air after serious museums.  They loved the live exhibits and were entertained by a real cat fight. The furnishings and paintings were pretty good too.Truly memorable.

I'm a big cat lover and was so excited about going to this museum and seeing the real cats wandering around. But I was quite disappointed. The museum is very small - only a couple of rooms - with quite a random collection of cat pictures. One of the main focuses seemed to be a Dutch cartoonist I had never heard of, though there is also a Rembrandt and a Picasso. Even so we did the whole museum in about half an hour.I was on the look out for the real cats that live there (I think I read that there are three) as I had seen photos of them sitting on chairs in the museum and being fussed over by visitors. We did the whole museum and didn't see any of the real cats, and it was only when we were about to leave that I saw a cat through the window of an external door marked 'private'. We were right by the ticket desk so I mentioned that there was a cat that wanted to come in (obviously hoping they would let it in) and they responded "yes, it's fine outside". I think they took pity after seeing the disappointed expression on my face as they then let the cat in and it went and sat on a chair in the gift shop - so at least I could say I had seen a cat in the cat museum! I still think this is worth a stop if you are a real cat lover but the museum itself isn't that great.

The museum has three rooms with posters on the wall, two paintings, some statues and thats it! I  thought there must be more, but no this is it. There was a small temporary exhibtion of Fien Westendorp what is for us dutch people nice as she was an illustrator of dutch childrenbooks but for tourists from abroad slightly interesting. Very poor and with 6 Euros entrance fee a really rip of. Spend your money elsewhere.

I loved visiting The Cat Cabinet. As the proud owner of two cats I adore anything and everything to do with cats. The collection of feline art is great and I had found a card at my hotel which allows for any visitor of the museum a free gift which turns out to be a free postcard of your choice with was lovely.

This place was OK. I'm not a massive cat lover, but I thought this museum would be quirky and fun. It was both of these things, but it would have helped if there was some kind of guide or commentary giving information about the exhibits. As others have noted, it's quite small and looking round doesn't take long. The house is beautiful and the staff are friendly though.

I have seen many of the posters in the Cat Cabinet in books and they were magnificent in full size. The building is also very interesting and it is a wonderful way to spend and hour or two. I would recommend a visit for any cat lover and anyone who loves poster art.
cats are my fave things ever,but for the price theres not alot to see here really,pics and postcards,i thought it would be more interseting.

This is a charming building tucked away down the end Herengracht. It is worth visiting as it contains an odd colleciton of artefacts and artwork of a catty nature. Most visitors only spend about half an hour there so it may not be worth the entrance fee to some, about 6 euros as I recall.

I loved this quirky museum with its beautiful location, stunning roomss and fantasic collectiopn of cat art. Given that I love cats, I was already sold, but this really is a beautiful collection housed in an amazing building. Partcularly intersting  pieced are highlighted. I particularly enjoyed the advertising posters. The git shop is small but purrfectly formed with a wide range of posters and postcards

This museum / gallery is quite small and therefore a bit overpriced in my opinion. It only takes about 20 mins to go around unless you stare at every painting for ages! There are loads of pieces though, some by famous artists like Picasso and Rembrandt. Some interesting sculptures too, but all in all probably wouldn't do it again.

I was surprised at how much fun you can have in a cat museum. There are some really nice bits of art. The gift shop is great, you can buy prints of the works at a reasonable price. The best part was the real cats.

Despite visiting Amsterdam several times I never knew this place existed.  My husband saved it as a surprise and it was fantastic.  the curators lounge around - happy to be stroked by cat sick owners.There's everything cat here so a good way to while away an hour or so.

Being an cat lover extraordinaire myself I am also kind of a cat tourist. And this is very lovely house, worth seeing just because of the interior. But I was busy petting the beutiful cats of the museum...They accepted it gracefully.
Funny concept to make a meseum around cats. Was it a tax driven development by a private person. Probably, yes! But still fun.

Little museum concentrating on Kitty Cats.  A lot of the art is paintings by famous artists.  Nice little gift shop.  I actually found two post cards of a painting of a cat and both looked like my kitty!  So bought them and framed them.   Must see if you are a cat lover!
Much better and more informative than we expected. Interesting posters, lovely paintings. You can even find cats sleeping on the sofa, they love people. Go for it if you like cats :)

One block from Banks Mansion hotel.  If you are a cat enthusiast, stop in for a quick (15-20 minutes) diversion - all kinds of art, posters, photographs, sculptures (and a very unique pinball table) of cats in a lovely canal house.  Small but we felt it was worth the 6 Euro entry fee to see an eccentric collection like this.

The Cat Cabinet is a sweet little gallery not far from the city centre. Don't go there expecting a huge show. It's a small, compact gallery with the main part taking up the first floor of a luxurious building. Amongst the decorative mouldings & chandeliers are posters, paintings, ornaments & sculptures celebrating cats & kittens. Not to mention several real cats snoozing away on the couches, completely oblivious to the tourists around them.The gift shop sells a plethora of cat related postcards as well as prints & posters.It cost us 6 Euros each and took us about 30 minutes to wander round. It was a pleasant way to escape the rain.

it's an old house (v beautiful house), full of cat posters, cat ornaments, anything feline - incl a cat which lives there and mooches about - it's an oddity for people who like things like the museum of everything in london, aswell as those who love cats.

Yes, this museum is not part of the Iamsterdam or Museumkaart but as a cat lover was worth the €6.  The museum is housed in one of the beautiful old canal homes and is loaded with cat related art and objects.  The great thing about this museum is the resident cats that are there on the sofa at the entrance as well as other locations throughout the house.  One of the cats even followed me room to room as if it was giving me a tour…LOL!  Nice to get some cat interaction while away from my own felines!Not too far from Central Station is the De Poezenboot, a cat shelter that was a converted house boat….check it out if you have time!!  Great group of people taking care of the cats, including Rock who is behind the counter.

This is not worth the entrance fee. Of all the museums and galleries that I went to (during 2 weeks in Netherlands and Belgium) this was the worst value for money.I like cats but 90% of the items on show are just posters and prints. There are some interesting pieces such as the pin-ball machine but there is so much more in Amsterdam that you should see before visiting here.

Visiting this place was one of the highlights of my trip to Amsterdam, and its so quirky its imposable not to like. Entrance was 5euros, and we were greeted by the owner who was very friendly and happy to chat. She explained that the first floor was dedicated to cat themed art, and then there was a room on the ground floor that had various prints you could buy. i loved how laid back everything was here, its a magnificent house which is just as interesting in the art itself-which was an incredible collection. there were a few cats roaming around looking very content. If this place had a little coffee area it would be perfect, i really just felt like spending all day in the beautiful house.

I give this place a 5 simply because there is no other like it in the world. Housed in a 17th century building whose residents include American president John Adam and a mayor of Amsterdam, there are live cats, 4 at last count, along with vintage feline commercial art posters (one by Toulouse Lautrec), sculptures, paintings and poems. Many of them I had only seen previously in art books and calendars are presented in a glorious backdrop. To add to the atmosphere, though others might disagree with me, the house is ever so slightly permeated by the scent of cat litter - a real mark of a cat house! Before you leave have a look at the period lavatory on the first floor. It could be the only one in an illustrious canal house open to the public. Check out the website http://www.kattenkabinet.nl/english.html

Ok, I am a cat lover, 2 of my own and have always had cat's in my life, but this is not a museum, its a galley fo some cat figures and some cat posters. There are 3 rooms upstairs, each with posters and a sofa, on each we found a sleeping cat, downstairs a room with cat posters to buy. Thats it, if you spend an hour here you are sheletering from the rain. Cannot undertsnad anyone mentioning how nice the rooms were, it is supposed to be about cats.Very dissapointed, sorry but honesty is the best policy.

If you're cat mad, this is a must. A tiny little place - takes 15 minutes to walk round - but worth seeing, just for the whole bizarreness of it! And if you're missing a kitty fix whilst on holidays, head here where you can stroke the feline residents to your heart's content. Lots of lovely posters for sale in the gift shop, so take some spending money!

It's definitely worth a visit especially for cat lovers.The house itself is very beautifuly decorated, and the art collection dedicated to cats is very interesting too.There are 4 cats living there who are friendly to visitors, and it makes this place even more pleasant to visit.The place is about 5-7 minutes walking from the floating flower market.

OK - I am a cat lover and so is my husband.  We totally loved this museum.  The artwork is all cat related but includes paintings, sculptures, photos, etc.  We loved the fact that it was housed in a beautiful old canal house.  Almost felt like walking around someone's home - there were very few people there but staff was extremely friendly.  The absolute best part was the real live kitties wandering about the place - probably about a dozen. Most enjoyed being petted and when I sat on a bench, a few jumped up for some attention.  Definitely one of the best museum experiences I've ever had, and I've been to A LOT.  It is not your usual stuffy "don't touch" kind of place.  One thing I noticed after visiting this museum is that Amsterdam cats actually LOOK different than American cats - their faces are generally squatter/flatter and they have a slightly different body shape - I thought that was very cool.  If you are a cat fanatic like me, definitely check out the place.  Also, try visiting the cat shelter barge - orphan kitties looking for a home on a barge on one of the canals by the Ann Frank House - we left a nice donation for their care.

We visited the Kattenkabinet on a recent trip to Amsterdam, as being cat lovers ourselves, thought it would make an interesting visit.The museum consists of posters, paintings and objets d'art of cats, with a couple of real-life ones mooching about the place. For us, it was slightly disappointing, as we thought it would actually be a museum to do with cats in terms of history, breeds, behaviour etc. However, we did enjoy the art itself, although if you're not into looking at picture after picture of various felines, then this might not be quite up your street!There is a small shop at the entrance selling posters for €5 each (which I thought were very nice), as well as books about the prints and paintings themselves. Worth a visit if you have an hour to spare and happen to be in the area, but the Poezenboot is another alternative for feline fanatics.

The main goal of this museum is an art collection showcasing cats from all different cultures and times using many mediums: cartoons, figurines, paintings, prints, sculptures- and there are live resident cats hanging out too.  The canal house that the museum is situated in is definately worth a tour- there are 5 rooms to go in, a ballroom from 1750, a dining room, a music room , a gallery, and a library.  These are ornate and splendid in their own right.  The woodwork is wonderful.  Check out the little bathroom at the end of the hall!  The lady working the desk was super friendly and didn't mind chatting with us.  Although not fully covered by the museumcaart, you get a discount if you have it.  It is only 5 euros anyway.If you hate cats- this is not the place for you.  If you like them, this will surely satisfy you.  Even after our tour of the red light district this place is still our top contender for most unique place we visited in Amsterdam.
